proposals.title: Supramolecular Peptide Amphiphile Nanofibers Hybridized with Natural ECM Polymers for Enhanced Chondrogenic Differentiation
proposals.motivation: Natural extracellular matrix (ECM) polymers like hyaluronic acid and collagen provide essential biochemical and structural cues but often lack the precise, tunable bioactivity of synthetic supramolecular systems. Conversely, synthetic peptide amphiphile (PA) nanofibers, while highly bioactive and programmable, may not fully replicate the complex, multifunctional environment of native ECM. A hybrid material that integrates the best of both worlds is needed to create superior scaffolds for regenerative medicine, particularly for challenging tissues like cartilage.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the co-assembly of chondrogenic PA nanofibers (e.g., TGF-β mimetic cyclic PAs) with natural ECM polymers will create a synergistic hybrid matrix that outperforms either component alone in directing stem cell fate and promoting functional tissue regeneration.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a library of chondrogenic PAs bearing charged or hydrogen-bonding motifs. Using established self-assembly protocols (pH switch, ionic strength), we will co-assemble these PAs with natural polymers (hyaluronic acid, chondroitin sulfate) in varying ratios. Co-assembly dynamics and nanostructure will be characterized by cryo-TEM, SAXS, and rheology. The bioactivity of the resulting hybrid nanofiber matrices will be tested using high-content single-cell analysis of human mesenchymal stem cell chondrogenesis, assessing differentiation markers (SOX9, collagen II) and matrix deposition.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish design rules for creating synergistic synthetic-natural ECM hybrids, moving beyond simple blending to integrated co-assembled nanostructures. It will provide a new class of biomaterials with enhanced bioactivity and mechanical integrity for cartilage repair, and offer fundamental insights into how synthetic and biological components communicate through supramolecular architecture to direct cell behavior.
proposals.novelty: While PA-ECM blends exist, the intentional, molecular-level co-assembly of PAs with natural polymers to create a unified, nanostructured hybrid matrix is a novel strategy. This approach leverages the Stupp lab's expertise in supramolecular design and single-cell coating to probe emergent bioactivity not accessible in phase-separated composites.

proposals.title: Supramolecular Design of Peptide Amphiphile Gels with Cartilage-Mimetic Stiffness and Stress Relaxation for Chondrogenic Differentiation
proposals.motivation: Articular cartilage is a complex viscoelastic tissue with a unique combination of compressive stiffness and time-dependent stress relaxation, both of which are critical mechanical cues for chondrocyte phenotype and matrix production. Current synthetic hydrogels for cartilage repair, including many peptide amphiphile (PA) systems, often focus on static elastic modulus, failing to recapitulate the dynamic mechanical environment. This mismatch may limit the efficacy of PA-based biomaterials in promoting sustained chondrogenesis and functional tissue regeneration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically tuning the supramolecular architecture and network dynamics of self-assembled PA gels through molecular design and co-assembly, we can create materials that independently modulate their equilibrium compressive modulus and stress relaxation rate to match native cartilage, thereby enhancing chondrogenic bioactivity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of PAs with variations in hydrophobic tail length, peptide sequence (integrating charged, polar, and β-sheet-forming residues), and crosslinkable moieties (e.g., methacrylate). Gel mechanical properties will be tuned via: 1) blending PAs of different stiffness potentials, 2) incorporating non-assembling, flexible polymer spacers (e.g., PEG-conjugated PAs) to control network dynamics, and 3) employing orthogonal photo-crosslinking to lock in nanostructure and modulate viscoelasticity. Rheology (frequency sweeps, stress relaxation, large amplitude oscillatory shear) and AFM-based nanoindentation will characterize stiffness and viscoelasticity. Structure-property relationships will be linked to nanostructure via cryo-TEM and SAXS.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design framework for decoupling stiffness and stress relaxation in supramolecular biomaterials, moving beyond elastic modulus as a sole design parameter. It will provide fundamental insight into how nanoscale network dynamics dictate macroscopic viscoelasticity in PA gels. The resulting cartilage-mimetic gels will serve as advanced platforms to study mechanotransduction in chondrogenesis and inform the next generation of injectable, bioactive materials for cartilage repair.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has tuned PA gel stiffness, the deliberate and independent programming of stress relaxation dynamics through controlled supramolecular co-assembly and network defect engineering represents a novel, biomimetic strategy. This approach integrates concepts from polymer physics into supramolecular design to capture the time-dependent mechanical signature of native tissue.

proposals.title: Supramolecular Assembly of Peptide Amphiphiles via Hydrophobic and Aromatic Interactions in Same-Charge or Neutral Systems
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 supramolecular biomaterials, particularly peptide amphiphiles (PAs), predominantly rely on complementary electrostatic interactions between oppositely charged residues to drive self-assembly and form bioactive nanofibers. This paradigm limits the chemical space of usable sequences, complicates co-assembly predictions, and can lead to nonspecific ionic interactions in biological environments. Exploring assembly driven purely by hydrophobic packing, π-π stacking, and hydrogen bonding, without net charge complementarity, would expand design rules for more predictable and stable nanostructures in physiological conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that peptide amphiphiles bearing the same net charge (e.g., all anionic) or neutral hydrophilic heads can be designed to self-assemble into well-defined, bioactive nanofibers through precise tuning of hydrophobic tail packing, β-sheet forming sequences, and aromatic interactions, circumventing the classical requirement for electrostatic complementarity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of PAs with identical anionic (glutamate) or neutral (PEG or serine) headgroups, systematically varying hydrophobic tail length (C10-C16) and incorporating aromatic (F, W, Y) or aliphatic (V, I, L) residues in the β-sheet domain. Assembly will be triggered by charge screening (e.g., physiological salt) or solvent exchange. Nanostructure morphology will be characterized by cryo-TEM and SAXS, while secondary structure and thermodynamics will be analyzed via CD and ITC. Co-assembly and self-sorting behaviors will be studied using fluorescently labeled PAs and FRET. Bioactivity will be tested by coating these nanofibers as extracellular matrix mimics for mesenchymal stem cell culture, assessing chondrogenic differentiation without exogenous growth factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish new design principles for charge-independent supramolecular biomaterials, providing a roadmap for creating stable nanostructures in high-ionic-strength environments. It will demonstrate that complex bioactivity (e.g., growth factor mimetic signaling) can be encoded purely through spatial presentation of epitopes on a neutrally charged scaffold, reducing off-target interactions. The findings will advance the field towards more predictable, modular, and translationally relevant self-assembling systems.
proposals.novelty: While self-sorting in mixed-charge systems is known, the deliberate design of homogeneous, same-charge PAs that assemble into robust nanofibers without any electrostatic attraction is a significant departure from established dogma. The approach uniquely leverages synergistic hydrophobic/aromatic forces to overcome electrostatic repulsion, a concept not fully explored in bioactive PA design pre-2026.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Supramolecular Ordering of Peptide Amphiphiles in Non-Aqueous Solvents: A Pathway to Hybrid Organic-Electronic Materials
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, supramolecular assembly of peptide amphiphiles (PAs) is predominantly studied in aqueous environments, driven by hydrophobic collapse and hydrogen bonding, limiting applications to biomedicine and aqueous-phase materials. Expanding assembly into organic solvents (e.g., DMSO, acetonitrile) or ionic liquids could unlock new hierarchical structures governed by distinct intermolecular forces, enabling the creation of hybrid organic-electronic or catalytic materials incompatible with water. This bridges Stupp's expertise in bioactive supramolecular systems with the underexplored domain of functional non-aqueous soft materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that peptide amphiphiles containing carefully balanced hydrogen-bonding motifs and solvophobic segments will undergo hierarchical self-assembly in select organic and ionic liquid solvents, yielding nanostructures with long-range order and distinct morphologies not accessible in water.
proposals.method: We will design a library of PAs with cyclic peptide heads (inspired by TGF-β1 mimetics) and alkyl tails, systematically varying peptide sequence (e.g., β-sheet propensity) and tail length. Assembly will be triggered in solvents of varying polarity (e.g., DMSO, DMF, ionic liquids like [BMIM][BF4]) by solvent-switch or cooling. Structures will be characterized by cryo-TEM, SAXS, and FTIR to determine morphology and internal order. We will employ the 'self-sorting vs coassembly' framework to study multi-component systems in these media, using fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) pairs to monitor mixing fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish fundamental design rules for supramolecular assembly in non-aqueous environments, expanding the materials chemistry of peptide amphiphiles beyond bioactivity. It will demonstrate a new class of solvent-processable, nanostructured hybrid materials with potential for organic electronics or heterogeneous catalysis. The findings will provide a critical bridge between biological supramolecular chemistry and non-biological soft matter engineering.
proposals.novelty: While solvent-mediated assembly is known, achieving the precise, hierarchical long-range order characteristic of Stupp's aqueous PA systems in non-aqueous solvents is non-trivial and largely unexplored. The deliberate translation of bio-inspired self-assembly principles to organic and ionic liquid media to create non-biological functional materials represents a significant conceptual and methodological leap.

proposals.title: Supramolecular Peptide-Amphiphile Sacs as Dynamic, Bioactive Microenvironments for Single-Cell Encapsulation and 3D Tissue Assembly
proposals.motivation: Current cell culture and tissue engineering platforms often lack the dynamic, three-dimensional, and bioactive complexity of native extracellular matrices (ECMs). Pre-2026, there is a recognized need for synthetic, programmable microenvironments that can protect cells, present specific signals, and adapt to cellular remodeling, which is crucial for advanced regenerative therapies and in vitro disease models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that self-assembling peptide amphiphiles (PAs) can be engineered to form stable, hollow sac-like compartments that encapsulate individual cells or small cell clusters, providing a protective, bioactive, and dynamically responsive 3D niche that directs cell fate and facilitates tissue assembly.
proposals.method: We will design a library of cyclic and linear PAs with tailored amphiphilicity and bioactive epitopes (e.g., TGF-β1 mimetics, RGD). Using solvent exchange and controlled pH/ionic triggers, we will drive the self-assembly of these PAs into hollow, vesicle-like sacs. Single-cell encapsulation will be achieved via microfluidic co-flow of cell suspensions with PA solutions. The structural integrity, permeability, and dynamic exchange of sac components will be characterized using cryo-TEM, SAXS, and fluorescence recovery after photobleaching (FRAP). Encapsulated cell viability, phenotype (e.g., chondrogenesis), and ability to form interconnected tissues will be assessed in vitro.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of supramolecular biomaterials that function as artificial, cell-sized ECM compartments. It will provide fundamental insights into how nanoscale molecular design dictates the formation of stable macroscopic sacs and their interaction with living cells. The technology could enable new strategies for single-cell analysis, cryopreservation, and the bottom-up construction of complex tissues.
proposals.novelty: While PA nanofibers are well-established for 3D cell culture, the deliberate engineering of PA self-assembly into stable, cell-encapsulating sacs represents a distinct and unexplored architectural paradigm. The integration of bioactive, dynamically exchangeable components within a protective shell creates a 'privileged' microenvironment not achievable with static scaffolds or bulk hydrogels.

proposals.title: Supramolecular Sacs as Programmable Synthetic Organelles for High-Throughput Single-Cell Secretion Analysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, there is a critical need for biological assays that better mimic the compartmentalized, crowded, and dynamic environment of the cell. Traditional in vitro assays often fail to capture the spatial organization and local concentration effects fundamental to cellular signaling and metabolic pathways. The Stupp lab's recent development of self-assembled, membrane-bound sacs from peptide amphiphiles provides a unique, bioinspired platform to address this gap by creating synthetic, chemically programmable compartments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that peptide amphiphile-based supramolecular sacs can be functionalized to act as synthetic organelles, enabling the compartmentalization and detection of specific cellular outputs, such as secreted proteins or metabolites, with single-cell resolution and enhanced sensitivity.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize peptide amphiphiles (PAs) bearing functional headgroups that specifically bind target analytes (e.g., cytokines, growth factors). These PAs will co-assemble into sac structures capable of encapsulating individual cells within high-density arrays. The binding of secreted analytes to the sac's interior will be coupled to a fluorescent or colorimetric readout via Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) pairs or enzymatic amplification integrated into the PA design. We will employ high-content imaging and microfluidics to analyze thousands of individual cell-sac compartments in parallel, quantifying secretion kinetics and heterogeneity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of biological assays that leverage supramolecular compartmentalization for spatially resolved, single-cell analysis of secretion and metabolic activity. It will provide a versatile platform to study cell-cell communication, immune responses, and stem cell differentiation with unprecedented contextual fidelity. The project will also advance fundamental understanding of how synthetic matrices can be engineered to interact with and report on complex biological processes.
proposals.novelty: While existing methods like droplet microfluidics provide compartmentalization, our approach uniquely integrates the dynamic, biomimetic, and signal-amplifying properties of supramolecular nanomaterials directly into the assay architecture. This moves beyond passive containers to create active, responsive synthetic organelles that participate in the biochemical process being measured.

proposals.title: Microfluidic Control of Supramolecular Membrane Interfaces for Programmable Bioactive Material Assembly
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular membranes formed by peptide amphiphiles (PAs) and hybrid polymers are central to creating bioactive scaffolds for tissue engineering and cell signaling. However, conventional bulk assembly methods lack spatiotemporal control over membrane contact and growth, limiting the ability to engineer complex, hierarchical interfaces with defined bioactivity. Microfluidics offers a powerful, yet underexplored, tool to precisely manipulate these self-assembling systems at the microscale, enabling the formation of sophisticated biomimetic structures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that microfluidic control of flow and confinement will enable the precise formation and guided growth of supramolecular membranes at defined interfaces, allowing for the programmable assembly of hybrid materials with tunable architecture and bioactivity.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate PDMS-based microfluidic devices with flow-focusing and droplet-generation geometries. Using pre-2026 knowledge of Stupp lab PA and polymer systems (e.g., TGF-β mimetic PAs, amphiphilic polymers), we will introduce aqueous streams containing molecular precursors into controlled oil phases or adjacent laminar flows. By precisely tuning flow rates, viscosities, and channel surface chemistry, we will direct the self-assembly of nanofibers and membranes at the liquid-liquid or liquid-solid interface. Real-time analysis will be performed using integrated microscopy and spectroscopy to monitor nanostructure formation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new methodology for the spatiotemporally controlled assembly of supramolecular biomaterials, moving beyond bulk coacervation. It will provide fundamental insights into how confinement and shear flow direct supramolecular self-sorting versus co-assembly. The resulting programmable membrane interfaces could lead to advanced scaffolds with patterned bioactivity for applications in neural, cartilage, or vascular tissue engineering.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics is used for polymer particle synthesis, its application to actively guide and control the interfacial assembly of peptide amphiphile supramolecular membranes—integrating Stupp's expertise in bioactive nanostructures with precise fluidic manipulation—is a novel and non-obvious synergy. This approach moves from passive self-assembly to actively engineered supramolecular morphogenesis.

proposals.title: Supramolecular Peptide Amphiphile Micro-Sacs for Spatiotemporally Controlled Drug Delivery
proposals.motivation: Current drug delivery systems often lack precise control over the timing and location of therapeutic release, leading to suboptimal efficacy and off-target effects. Supramolecular nanostructures, such as those pioneered by the Stupp lab, offer dynamic, stimuli-responsive platforms, but their application has been largely limited to macroscopic gels or nanoscale fibers. Creating defined, self-assembled microscopic sacs would bridge a critical gap in the length scale of delivery vehicles, enabling new strategies for localized, sustained release.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that peptide amphiphiles (PAs) can be designed to self-assemble into stable, hollow, microscopic sacs via controlled interfacial co-assembly, and that these micro-sacs can encapsulate and release therapeutic agents in response to specific biological stimuli.
proposals.method: We will design a library of PAs with varying hydrophobic block lengths and bioactive epitopes (e.g., enzyme-cleavable or pH-sensitive sequences) to modulate self-assembly energetics. Micro-sacs will be fabricated using a water-in-oil emulsion templating technique, where the aqueous phase contains the PAs and a therapeutic cargo (e.g., a small molecule drug or protein). The oil-water interface will direct the co-assembly of PAs into a cohesive, sac-like membrane. We will characterize sac morphology (SEM, cryo-TEM), stability, encapsulation efficiency, and triggered release profiles in vitro using relevant enzymatic or acidic conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of stimuli-responsive, supramolecular micro-carriers with precisely tunable membrane properties derived from molecular design. It will demonstrate a scalable method to create hierarchical structures from peptide amphiphiles, expanding their utility beyond nanofibers and bulk gels. The resulting platform could enable more effective delivery of drugs for conditions like cancer or localized inflammation.
proposals.novelty: While PA nanofibers and macroscopic gels are well-established, the creation of discrete, hollow micro-sacs via interfacial self-assembly represents a novel architectural control. This approach uniquely combines the dynamic, bioactive nature of supramolecular PAs with the encapsulation capacity and size regime of microparticles, a combination not yet explored in the field.

proposals.title: Electrostatically Coassembled Nanostructures from Short Polyelectrolytes and Large Peptide Amphiphiles for Tunable Bioactivity
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular coassembly of oppositely charged species is a powerful route to functional biomaterials, but typically employs large polymers with small molecules. This conventional approach limits control over nanostructure hierarchy and the density of bioactive epitopes. Inverting the size relationship, as proposed here, could yield novel, kinetically trapped nanostructures with emergent properties for directing cell behavior, a core challenge in regenerative medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using a small, charged polymer (e.g., a short, linear poly-L-lysine) as a multivalent electrostatic scaffold for large, self-assembling oppositely charged peptide amphiphiles (PAs) will produce non-equilibrium, kinetically controlled coassemblies with distinct nanostructural morphologies and enhanced bioactivity compared to systems where the polymer is the larger component.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of large, anionic PAs bearing bioactive epitopes (e.g., laminin or TGF-β mimetic cyclic peptides) and coassemble them with short cationic poly-L-lysine (PLL, ~10-20 mer) in aqueous solution. Nanostructure formation will be characterized by cryo-TEM, SAXS, and CD spectroscopy. Coassembly kinetics and thermodynamic stability will be probed via time-resolved fluorescence and ITC. Bioactivity will be assessed using high-content imaging of single cells coated with the resulting nanofiber matrices to quantify chondrogenic or neuronal differentiation markers.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for electrostatic coassembly, moving beyond polymer-dominated systems. It will provide fundamental insights into how kinetic trapping in supramolecular systems can be engineered to create hybrid materials with tunable nanostructures and mechanical properties. The resulting platforms could offer superior control over the spatial presentation of signals to cells.
proposals.novelty: The inversion of the conventional size relationship in electrostatic coassembly is a non-obvious strategy to access non-equilibrium nanostructures. Combining Stupp lab's expertise in bioactive PAs with the use of short polymers as dynamic scaffolds, rather than static templates, represents a significant departure from existing polymer-peptide hybrid material designs.

proposals.title: Decoupling Bioactive Signal Presentation from Scaffold Architecture: A Comparative Study of IKVAV Display on Engineered Self-Assembling Nanomaterials
proposals.motivation: The IKVAV epitope from laminin, presented on peptide amphiphile (PA) nanofibers, is a potent inducer of neural differentiation. However, it remains unclear whether the bioactivity is intrinsic to the IKVAV sequence or is critically dependent on the specific supramolecular architecture of the PA nanofiber. Disentangling the effects of epitope density, nanoscale presentation, and scaffold mechanics is essential for rational biomaterial design. This study will challenge the paradigm of the PA as the only optimal scaffold for IKVAV.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the neural differentiation outcomes driven by the IKVAV signal are not unique to the PA nanofiber scaffold and can be replicated or even enhanced by presenting IKVAV at high density on alternative self-assembling nanostructures with distinct geometries and mechanical properties.
proposals.method: We will synthesize IKVAV-functionalized monomers for three distinct self-assembling systems: 1) β-sheet forming peptides (e.g., Fmoc- or Nap-based) forming fibrillar hydrogels, 2) DNA nanostructures (e.g., tile-based assemblies) offering precise geometric control, and 3) synthetic polymer amphiphiles (e.g., PEG-PLA) forming spherical micelles. Using established conjugation chemistries (e.g., click, NHS-ester), we will ensure high and quantifiable epitope density. We will characterize nanostructure morphology (TEM, Cryo-EM), mechanical properties (rheology), and IKVAV presentation (fluorescence spectroscopy, ELISA). Neural progenitor cells (e.g., PC-12 or primary neural stem cells) will be cultured on these materials, and differentiation (neurite outgrowth, β-III tubulin expression) will be quantified and compared to standard IKVAV-PA controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental structure-activity relationship for a key bioactive signal, clarifying the relative contributions of molecular identity versus material context. It will expand the toolkit of supramolecular biomaterials by validating new scaffolds for neural tissue engineering. The findings will provide generalizable design principles for presenting bioactive epitopes in regenerative medicine.
proposals.novelty: While IKVAV-PAs are well-studied, a systematic comparison of its bioactivity across fundamentally different, yet precisely engineered, self-assembling platforms has not been performed. The use of DNA nanostructures for high-density peptide presentation in a neural context is particularly novel and leverages precise spatial control unavailable in traditional supramolecular polymers.

proposals.title: Programmable DNA Origami Lattices as Supramolecular Templates for Systematic Investigation of Epitope Spacing and Pattern on Neural Cell Signaling
proposals.motivation: The spatial presentation of bioactive epitopes, such as the IKVAV peptide from laminin, is a critical but poorly understood parameter in designing biomaterials for neural regeneration. While supramolecular assemblies like peptide amphiphile nanofibers allow epitope display, they lack the precise, tunable, and long-range order needed to dissect how specific nanoscale patterns influence receptor clustering and signaling. A platform for creating defined two-dimensional epitope arrays with atomic precision is needed to move beyond empirical design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using DNA origami as a programmable lattice template to create IKVAV peptide arrays with precisely controlled spacing and symmetry will reveal distinct thresholds and optimal patterns for neural cell adhesion and mechanotransduction signaling, which cannot be accessed with stochastically presented epitopes.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize 2D DNA origami sheets (e.g., using a square or honeycomb lattice from the caDNAno software library) with periodic attachment sites for IKVAV-conjugated oligonucleotides. Lattice constants will be systematically varied (10, 20, 50 nm). A second design will incorporate intentional line defects. These DNA-epitope hybrid materials will be characterized by AFM and TEM. Their interaction with neural progenitor cells will be assessed via high-content imaging for adhesion and morphology, and immunofluorescence/FRET for downstream signaling (e.g., FAK, YAP localization).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel hybrid material platform combining the programmability of DNA nanotechnology with the bioactivity of peptide epitopes. It will provide the first systematic, quantitative map linking defined epitope lattice geometry to specific cellular responses, offering fundamental design principles for next-generation bioactive materials in regenerative medicine.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its conceptual adaptation of photonic crystal engineering principles to create precisely patterned biochemical landscapes, and in its technical fusion of Stupp lab supramolecular peptide expertise with the atomic-precision templating of DNA origami, enabling experiments previously impossible with self-assembling peptides alone.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Screening of Dynamic Synthetic Microenvironments via Clickable Microfluidic Nanofiber Droplets
proposals.motivation: Current biomaterial screening platforms predominantly present static biochemical cues, failing to capture the dynamic reciprocity of native extracellular matrices where signaling is temporally regulated. This static presentation limits our understanding of how the sequence and timing of biochemical signals, such as growth factors or adhesion motifs, direct cell fate decisions, migration, and therapeutic responses. A platform enabling real-time, post-encapsulation manipulation of scaffold biochemistry in a high-throughput format is critically needed to decode these dynamic cellular processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dynamic, post-fabrication modification of peptide amphiphile (PA) nanofiber scaffolds within single-cell microdroplets, via bioorthogonal click chemistry, will reveal that the temporal sequence of biochemical cue presentation is a critical determinant of stem cell chondrogenesis and drug sensitivity, outcomes not predictable from static presentation alone.
proposals.method: We will integrate droplet microfluidics with supramolecular nanofiber design. First, we will synthesize self-assembling peptide amphiphiles (PAs) bearing inert tetrazine or trans-cyclooctene handles. These click-PAs will form nanofiber matrices within picoliter aqueous droplets, co-encapsulating single mesenchymal stem cells. The droplets will be arrayed in a microfluidic chip or collected for imaging. Subsequently, we will perfuse the array with complementary click-functionalized ligands (e.g., TGF-β1 mimetic cyclic peptides, RGD, or drug conjugates) to dynamically 'click-in' specific signals at defined timepoints. Cell responses (e.g., chondrogenic markers via immunofluorescence, morphology, viability) will be tracked in high-throughput using automated microscopy and image analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel high-throughput screening platform for 4D cell culture, enabling the systematic exploration of dynamic extracellular matrix signaling. It will generate fundamental knowledge on how temporal changes in scaffold biochemistry direct stem cell differentiation, potentially identifying optimal sequences for tissue regeneration. The platform will also provide a powerful tool for studying dynamic cell-drug interactions within tunable synthetic microenvironments.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of droplet-based single-cell encapsulation with post-assembly, bioorthogonal functionalization of supramolecular nanofibers creates a uniquely dynamic and high-throughput screening environment. This moves beyond static co-assembly or pre-functionalized systems, allowing for real-time, sequential editing of the cellular niche with precise temporal control, which is non-obvious from existing single-coating or self-sorting approaches.

proposals.title: Active Mechanistic Dissection of Biomimetic Mineralization: A Combinatorial Nanofiber and In-Situ Perturbation Strategy
proposals.motivation: The controlled nucleation of hydroxyapatite (HA) on synthetic materials is critical for bone regeneration, yet current design is largely empirical. Pre-2026 work, including Stupp's on peptide amphiphile nanofibers, shows bioactivity but lacks a causative understanding of how specific surface features dictate nucleation kinetics and crystal orientation. Moving beyond descriptive correlations to predictive design requires a platform that actively probes and deconvolutes the energetic contributions of individual surface parameters.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the nucleation barrier and crystallographic orientation of hydroxyapatite are dictated by discrete, quantifiable interactions between specific surface chemical motifs (e.g., carboxylate clusters, phosphorylated residues) and the pre-nucleation cluster solution chemistry, which can be isolated through systematic surface variation and in-situ perturbation.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a combinatorial library of electrospun nanofibers using hybrid polymer-peptide amphiphile systems to systematically vary surface chemistry (functional group identity, density, spatial patterning) and topography. Mineralization will be actively probed using synchronized in-situ liquid-phase transmission electron microscopy (LP-TEM) and synchrotron-based microbeam small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS). Deliberate perturbations—such as pulsed injection of calcium or phosphate chelators, or pH jumps—will be introduced during time-resolved data collection to test the stability of nascent clusters and quantify the energetic role of specific surface features.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a causative, rather than correlative, link between designed material surfaces and mineralization outcomes. It will yield a quantitative framework of surface interaction energies that govern nucleation, providing concrete design principles for the next generation of predictive osteoinductive biomaterials. The integrated combinatorial and active-perturbation methodology will serve as a new paradigm for studying complex interfacial processes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the shift from passive observation to active mechanistic dissection via synchronized in-situ perturbations during nucleation on a combinatorially designed surface library. This integrated approach, combining high-throughput material synthesis with real-time energetic probing, is unprecedented in biomimetic mineralization studies pre-2026.

proposals.title: Topologically Programmed Supramolecular Networks for Creep-Free, Reversibly Assembled Metamaterials
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular materials offer dynamic, stimuli-responsive properties but are plagued by creep—irreversible flow under sustained load—which limits their use in load-bearing applications. Overcoming this requires moving beyond simple associative polymer physics to architecturally designed networks that mimic the toughness of biological tissues, which combine strength and self-healing. This project addresses the critical need for synthetic materials that possess covalent-like mechanical integrity yet retain full reversibility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical, percolated network architecture, constructed from precisely arranged strong supramolecular motifs, will channel mechanical stress into localized zones of reversible bond reformation, thereby suppressing macroscopic creep while maintaining the capacity for full, on-demand disassembly.
proposals.method: We will synthesize peptide amphiphiles (PAs) functionalized with strong, directional motifs: β-cyclodextrin/adamantane host-guest pairs for shear-resistant junctions and terpyridine/metal-ion complexes (e.g., Fe²⁺) for energy-dissipative clusters. Using controlled pH and ionic strength, we will guide these PAs into nanofibers. A secondary self-assembly step, templated by a sacrificial polymer scaffold, will organize these fibers into a bicontinuous gyroid-like network, creating a percolated, stress-redistributing metamaterial. Rheology (creep-recovery tests) and in-situ scattering (SAXS/USAXS) under load will correlate topology to mechanical response.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design principle for achieving creep resistance through supramolecular topology, rather than covalent cross-linking. It will yield a new class of hybrid biomaterials with unprecedented mechanical stability for dynamic environments like cartilage repair, while providing a fundamental framework for stress management in non-covalent networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit topological programming of a supramolecular network to guide stress dissipation, moving beyond material chemistry to architectural design. This integrates concepts from metamaterials and polymer physics into supramolecular science to solve the long-standing creep problem.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Supramolecular Polymers as Gecko-Inspired, Reversibly Adhesive Biomaterials
proposals.motivation: Covalent cross-linking is the dominant paradigm for creating stable, load-bearing biomaterials, but it precludes dynamic remodeling, self-healing, and gentle integration with living tissues. Inspired by the gecko's hierarchical footpad, which achieves strong, reversible adhesion through collective van der Waals forces, this project seeks to create a new class of synthetic materials where mechanical integrity emerges from programmable non-covalent interactions. This could enable adaptive, stimuli-responsive materials for regenerative medicine and soft robotics that avoid the permanence and potential toxicity of covalent networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by engineering a multi-scale hierarchical architecture in peptide amphiphile (PA) assemblies—from molecular-scale β-sheet H-bonding to nanofiber entanglement to microscale patterning—we can create bulk materials with cohesive and adhesive strength rivaling covalently cross-linked gels, while retaining full reversibility and self-healing capability.
proposals.method: We will design a library of PAs with terminal motifs (e.g., Dopa, napthalene) to provide tunable, reversible interfacial adhesion (π-π stacking, metal coordination). Using established Stupp lab methods, we will drive these PAs into high-aspect-ratio nanofibers via pH or ionic trigger, exploiting β-sheet formation and hydrophobic collapse for primary stability. We will then use techniques like solvent evaporation, electrospinning, or templating on micropatterned surfaces to create controlled, hierarchical fiber networks. Mechanical properties (adhesion, toughness, self-healing) will be characterized via rheology and tensile testing, comparing performance to covalent PEG-diacrylate hydrogels.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept that hierarchical non-covalent design can replace covalent cross-links for specific material functions, contributing a new design principle to supramolecular materials science. It will yield a platform of dynamically adhesive biomaterials with potential for non-invasive tissue sealing or as injectable, self-assembling scaffolds for cell delivery. The findings will challenge the assumed necessity of covalent bonds in structural biomaterials.
proposals.novelty: While supramolecular polymers are known for their dynamics, the deliberate, multi-scale architectural design to *amplify* weak interactions into macroscopic, reversible mechanical integrity—directly inspired by the gecko adhesion mechanism—is a novel biomimetic strategy not yet fully explored in synthetic biomaterials.

proposals.title: Decoupling Morphology from Function: Anisotropic Mineralization via Programmed Co-Assembly of Non-Fibrous Peptide Amphiphiles
proposals.motivation: Biomineralization in tissues like bone and enamel relies on extracellular matrices providing long-range anisotropic cues to direct crystal growth, a function widely attributed to the static fibrous architecture of collagen. Supramolecular biomaterials often mimic this with persistent nanofibers, conflating shape with function. This project challenges that paradigm by asking if dynamic, non-fibrous order alone can direct mineralization, a fundamental question with implications for designing simpler, more adaptable synthetic biomaterials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a transient, pervasive anisotropic molecular field, generated by the programmed co-assembly of non-fibrous units into a shear-aligned or liquid crystalline phase, is sufficient to direct oriented crystal growth, even in the absence of a static fibrous scaffold.
proposals.method: We will design a library of peptide amphiphiles (PAs) with complementary charged, hydrogen-bonding, and hydrophobic motifs to drive co-assembly into non-fibrous micellar or bicontinuous phases. Using rheology-coupled small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS), we will screen for formulations that form shear-induced nematic or smectic liquid crystalline order. Mineralization will be initiated in situ within this dynamic matrix using simulated body fluid, and the orientation of hydroxyapatite crystals will be characterized via TEM and grazing-incidence wide-angle X-ray scattering (GIWAXS) to correlate with the transient supramolecular order.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for biomimetic mineralization, demonstrating that long-range directional control can be achieved through dynamic supramolecular order rather than static morphology. It will expand the toolkit for creating synthetic bone grafts by enabling materials that are injectable and self-ordering under physiological shear. The findings will provide a fundamental test of the structural determinants of biomineralization.
proposals.novelty: This approach is non-obvious because it deliberately eschews the well-established fibrous nanostructures known to template mineralization, instead testing if a dynamic, anisotropic field from simpler units is functionally equivalent. It leverages programmed co-assembly and shear alignment—concepts from soft matter physics—to create transient order for a biological process.

proposals.title: Dynamic Mechanical Control of Supramolecular Peptide Nanofiber Scaffolds via Tunable Covalent and Non-Covalent Cross-Linking
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular peptide amphiphile (PA) nanofibers are promising scaffolds for regenerative medicine, but their dynamic, non-covalent nature often limits mechanical stability under physiological loads. Conversely, permanent covalent cross-linking can lock the structure, eliminating adaptive and self-healing properties crucial for cell-scaffold interactions. There is a critical need for strategies to fine-tune cross-linking reversibility and kinetics to create scaffolds that are initially robust yet can remodel in response to cellular activity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating dynamic covalent chemistry (e.g., hydrazone, disulfide exchange) and photo-triggerable cross-linkers into the supramolecular design of PA nanofibers, we can create a hybrid system where the degree of cross-linking and its reversibility can be spatiotemporally controlled, leading to adaptive mechanical properties that guide specific cell behaviors.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of PAs functionalized with orthogonal reactive groups (e.g., aldehydes, hydrazides, cysteines, and caged thiols). These will co-assemble with standard bioactive PAs to form nanofibers. Cross-linking will be introduced via: 1) pH-dependent hydrazone bonds, 2) redox-responsive disulfide bridges, and 3) light-activated thiol-ene 'click' chemistry using a photoinitiator. Rheology will quantify the storage/loss moduli, self-healing, and stress relaxation kinetics. High-resolution microscopy (cryo-TEM, AFM) will assess nanostructural integrity. We will then coat these dynamic matrices onto single cells and 3D cultures to study chondrogenesis and matrix deposition.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design framework for dynamically cross-linked supramolecular biomaterials, providing quantitative relationships between cross-linker chemistry, network dynamics, and bulk mechanical properties. It will demonstrate how adaptive scaffold mechanics, beyond static stiffness, can be used as a directive signal for tissue morphogenesis.
proposals.novelty: While cross-linking of PA gels is known, the deliberate and independent tuning of cross-link density and reversibility kinetics within a single supramolecular architecture to achieve programmable mechanical adaptation is unexplored. This moves beyond binary (static/dynamic) systems to a continuum of controllable viscoelasticity.

proposals.title: Electrochemically Templated Mineralization via Ion-Selective Membranes and Supramolecular Peptide Amphiphile Matrices
proposals.motivation: Precise spatiotemporal control over biomineralization remains a significant challenge in creating synthetic bone grafts and dental composites that mimic the complex hierarchical structure of native tissue. Existing electrochemical methods lack the ion-specificity and localized concentration control needed for high-fidelity templating within soft, self-assembled matrices. Integrating principles from electrodialysis with supramolecular templating addresses this gap by enabling targeted ion delivery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a cation-exchange membrane (e.g., for Ca2+) with a self-assembled peptide amphiphile (PA) nanofiber matrix will provide exquisite control over local ion supersaturation, leading to accelerated and more structurally faithful mineralization of calcium phosphate phases within the organic template.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a custom electrochemical cell with a Ca2+-selective cation-exchange membrane separating the cathode chamber, which contains a PA hydrogel (e.g., a known osteogenic PA with phosphorylated serine residues). A tailored DC potential will be applied to selectively pump Ca2+ ions from a reservoir into the PA matrix. Concurrently, phosphate ions will be supplied via diffusion or a co-iontophoretic method. The mineralization process will be monitored in situ using time-lapse small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and electron microscopy to correlate ion flux with nucleation kinetics and crystal orientation relative to the PA nanofibers.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel electrochemical platform for spatially guided biomimetic mineralization, directly contributing to the field of hybrid organic-inorganic materials. It will demonstrate how membrane-based ion selectivity can overcome diffusion limitations and enable the formation of complex mineral composites with improved mechanical properties and bioactivity for regenerative medicine.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious integration of ion-selective membrane technology from desalination with supramolecular PA templates creates a new paradigm for electrochemical synthesis, moving beyond simple electrode reactions to achieve precise, membrane-mediated ionic control within a soft, bioactive scaffold.

proposals.title: Single-Fiber Mechano-Mineralization: Probing Nucleation Kinetics and Templating Forces on Isolated Peptide-Amphiphile Nanofibers
proposals.motivation: Understanding the initial nucleation and growth of minerals on supramolecular templates is crucial for designing biomimetic materials for bone regeneration and hard tissue engineering. However, ensemble studies of mineralization in bulk gels obscure the fundamental, nanoscale events of nucleation site preference and the mechanical interplay between a growing crystal and its soft organic template. A method to isolate and interrogate a single templating fiber is needed to uncover these basic principles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mineralization on a single peptide-amphiphile (PA) nan fiber initiates at specific, high-energy defect sites or functional group clusters, and that the growing mineral phase exerts quantifiable, site-specific mechanical forces on the supramolecular template, altering its local structure.
proposals.method: We will adapt single-molecule biophysics techniques to create a controlled, single-fiber mineralization assay. Specifically, a single PA nanofiber, synthesized to present known mineralization-directing sequences (e.g., phosphoserine), will be isolated and suspended between a functionalized AFM tip and a substrate or within a dual-beam optical trap. This isolated fiber will be immersed in a controlled mineralization solution (simulated body fluid). Using high-speed AFM or in-situ optical microscopy coupled with interferometric scattering, we will observe in real-time the location and kinetics of mineral nucleation. Concurrently, AFM force spectroscopy or trap stiffness measurements will quantify the mechanical forces and deformations imparted on the fiber during crystal growth.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, real-time measurements of mineralization kinetics on a defined supramolecular template, free from ensemble averaging. It will establish a causal link between nanofiber surface chemistry/defects and nucleation efficiency, and quantify the mechanical feedback between inorganic crystal and organic matrix at the nanoscale, informing the design of next-generation hybrid materials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of single-molecule force spectroscopy and manipulation paradigms—common in biophysics for studying proteins and DNA—to the fundamental problem of biomineralization on synthetic supramolecular scaffolds, a previously unexplored analytical approach in materials science.

proposals.title: Heteromeric Peptide Amphiphile Co-Assemblies: Programming Crystalline Order from Chemically Distinct Building Blocks
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular materials, particularly peptide amphiphiles (PAs), rely on molecular self-assembly for function, but design paradigms often assume or require chemical homogeneity for long-range order. This limits the complexity and multifunctionality of bioinspired materials. Challenging the necessity for identical blocks could unlock new classes of hybrid, hierarchically structured materials with emergent properties not found in homomeric systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing complementary, chemically distinct peptide amphiphile blocks with precisely matched steric and non-covalent interaction profiles, we can achieve co-assembly into nanostructures with internal crystalline order, rather than leading to disordered aggregates or self-sorted phases.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize two distinct PA libraries: one with a β-sheet-forming peptide sequence (e.g., VVVAAA) and another with a charged, bioactive sequence (e.g., RGDS). Using pre-2026 methods, we will systematically vary the hydrophobic tail length and linker chemistry to modulate packing. Co-assembly will be driven by solvent exchange and characterized by cryo-TEM, SAXS, and FTIR to assess nanostructure morphology and internal β-sheet crystallinity. Single-molecule fluorescence and FRET will quantify mixing fidelity at the molecular level, distinguishing true co-assembly from self-sorting.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for creating ordered, multicomponent supramolecular biomaterials. It will provide a foundational understanding of how to encode co-assembly instructions into molecular structure, moving beyond single-component systems. The resulting heteromeric nanostructures could serve as more sophisticated scaffolds for multi-signal cell signaling or hybrid organic-inorganic composites.
proposals.novelty: While self-sorting in mixtures is known, achieving a co-assembled crystalline phase from designed, chemically dissimilar blocks is non-trivial and underexplored. This approach deliberately engineers mismatch to create a new, higher-order composite material, contrasting with the common use of identical or highly similar blocks for crystallization.

proposals.title: Repulsive Electrostatic and Steric Forces as Deterministic Controllers of Finite Length in Peptide Amphiphile Nanofibers
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular polymers, such as peptide amphiphile (PA) nanofibers, exhibit finite assembly sizes critical for their bioactivity and materials properties. While attractive forces driving assembly are well-studied, the specific repulsive forces that terminate growth and dictate ultimate nanostructure dimensions remain poorly understood. A systematic investigation into these repulsive mediators is essential for achieving predictive, bottom-up control over supramolecular architecture, a fundamental challenge in materials science and nanomedicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the finite length of one-dimensional PA nanofibers is governed by a balance between cohesive intermolecular attractions and long-range electrostatic repulsions combined with terminal steric blockade, and that these repulsive forces can be rationally tuned via molecular design to program specific assembly sizes.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of PAs with systematically varied charged residues (e.g., glutamic acid, lysine) and sterically bulky end-groups (e.g., PEG chains, aromatic moieties) positioned at the terminus of the peptide sequence. Using cryogenic transmission electron microscopy (cryo-TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM), we will measure nanofiber length distributions. We will correlate these structural data with zeta potential measurements and computational modeling (e.g., coarse-grained molecular dynamics) of electrostatic and steric interactions. The assembly process will be monitored in real-time using in-situ small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) to track growth kinetics and termination.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative design principle for controlling supramolecular polymer size through engineered repulsion, moving beyond empirical approaches. It will provide a foundational framework for tailoring nanostructure dimensions for specific applications in regenerative medicine, such as modulating bioactivity in chondrogenesis or controlling cell-nanofiber interactions in single-cell coatings.
proposals.novelty: While self-sorting and co-assembly in PAs have been explored, explicitly decoupling and quantifying the role of repulsive electrostatic and steric forces as independent, tunable 'stop signals' for supramolecular growth is a novel and non-obvious strategy for size control.

proposals.title: Functional Polydisperse Films from Self-Sorting Peptide Amphiphile Libraries via Tunable Supramolecular Frustration
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular materials, especially peptide amphiphiles (PAs), are prized for their monodisperse, well-defined nanostructures, but this regularity limits their ability to mimic the structural heterogeneity and multifunctionality of native extracellular matrices. Pre-2026 work has shown that co-assembly and self-sorting can create complex systems, but the intentional design of polydisperse yet cohesive functional films remains an unexplored frontier for creating more robust and adaptive biomaterials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing a library of peptide amphiphiles with controlled structural mismatches—varying in alkyl tail length, peptide charge, and β-sheet propensity—we can induce a self-sorting, polydisperse co-assembly that yields cohesive, nanostructured films with emergent mechanical and bioactive properties not found in monodisperse systems.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a combinatorial library of PAs using solid-phase peptide synthesis, systematically varying hydrophobic segment length (C10-C16) and incorporating charged (e.g., E, K) and uncharged polar (e.g., S, Q) residues in the peptide segment to modulate intermolecular interactions. Assembly will be driven in aqueous solution, and the resulting nanostructures will be characterized by cryo-TEM and SAXS to quantify polydispersity. Film formation will be achieved via solvent casting or interfacial assembly, with mechanical properties assessed by AFM nanoindentation. Bioactivity will be evaluated by coating these films and studying single-cell adhesion and morphology using high-content imaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for 'functional polydispersity' in supramolecular materials, moving beyond the pursuit of perfect monodispersity. It will provide a framework for creating hybrid films that better emulate tissue complexity and could lead to more tunable platforms for cell culture and regenerative medicine.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in intentionally designing for polydispersity as a material feature, using controlled self-sorting within a PA library to create functional films, rather than viewing polydispersity as a defect to be eliminated or a simple binary co-assembly outcome.

proposals.title: Post-Assembly Molecular Editing of Peptide Amphiphile Nanostructures via Dynamic Covalent and Supramolecular Exchange
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular materials, such as peptide amphiphile (PA) nanostructures, are typically 'locked in' after their initial self-assembly, with control limited to the nucleation and growth phases. This severely restricts their functional adaptability post-synthesis, a critical limitation for applications in dynamic biological environments or responsive materials. Enabling post-synthetic restructuring of the microstructure would unlock new paradigms for adaptive biomaterials and self-healing systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating dynamic covalent chemistry (e.g., hydrazone or disulfide bonds) into PA molecular designs will allow for selective, post-assembly molecular editing of nanostructures via chemical or enzymatic triggers, thereby enabling controlled restructuring of their internal microstructure and emergent properties without complete disassembly.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of PAs bearing dynamic covalent linkers between the peptide epitope and alkyl tail, and co-assemble them with standard PAs. Using established high-resolution techniques (cryo-TEM, SAXS), we will characterize the initial nanostructures. Post-assembly, we will apply specific chemical triggers (pH change, reducing agents) or enzymes to cleave and reform the dynamic bonds. The restructuring process will be monitored in real-time via in-situ spectroscopy (CD, FTIR) and stopped-flow light scattering to track morphological transitions and molecular exchange kinetics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational methodology for the post-synthetic 'editing' of supramolecular biomaterials, moving beyond static architectures. It will provide new design rules for creating adaptive, stimuli-responsive nanostructures with tunable bioactivity. The findings will directly impact the development of next-generation injectable therapeutics and dynamic extracellular matrix mimics.
proposals.novelty: While molecular exchange in supramolecular systems is known, the deliberate use of dynamic covalent chemistry to *rewire* the internal molecular connectivity and hierarchy of a pre-formed nanostructure is a novel and non-obvious strategy. This goes beyond simple monomer exchange to achieve programmable microstructure metamorphosis.

proposals.title: Supramolecular Nanostructures from Sequence-Defined Synthetic Polymers for Programmable Bioactivity Beyond Peptide Mimetics
proposals.motivation: Biomimetic materials often rely on peptides or peptide amphiphiles to emulate biological macromolecules, inheriting challenges like enzymatic degradation, immunogenicity, and limited chemical diversity. A paradigm shift towards fully synthetic, sequence-defined polymers could offer superior control over nanostructure formation, stability, and bioactive signaling, enabling more robust and tunable biomaterials. This addresses a key limitation in supramolecular biomaterials by moving beyond direct imitation to achieve enhanced functionality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sequence-defined, non-biological polymers, when designed with amphiphilic blocks and bioinspired interactive motifs (e.g., sulfonates, guanidiniums), can self-assemble into bioactive supramolecular nanostructures that direct specific cellular responses with greater precision and stability than natural peptide-based systems.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of sequence-defined oligomers using iterative solid-phase synthesis, combining hydrophobic blocks (e.g., aromatic units) with charged or H-bonding motifs that mimic protein surface chemistry. These synthetic amphiphiles will be co-assembled with known bioactive peptide amphiphiles (PAs) or alone, and their self-assembly pathways, nanostructure morphology (via cryo-TEM, SAXS), and dynamic exchange (via FRET) will be characterized. Their bioactivity will be tested in high-throughput cell culture assays, focusing on chondrogenesis and stem cell differentiation, comparing directly to TGF-β1 mimetic PAs.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for bioactive supramolecular materials, demonstrating that precise bioactivity can be encoded in synthetic polymers without natural amino acids. It will provide a framework for creating enzymatically stable, chemically versatile biomaterials with programmable cell signaling. The results will expand the supramolecular toolbox for regenerative medicine beyond peptide and protein mimics.
proposals.novelty: The approach deliberately avoids biological building blocks, using synthetic polymer sequences to recapitulate and enhance bioactivity through supramolecular design, a significant departure from the field's focus on peptide mimics. It leverages Stupp lab expertise in co-assembly and dynamic nanostructures to create hybrid systems where synthetic polymers dictate assembly and stability while interfacing with biological environments.

proposals.title: Supramolecular Peptide Amphiphile Templates for Programmable Cavity Engineering in Hybrid Organic-Inorganic Semiconductors
proposals.motivation: The electronic and optical properties of semiconductors are profoundly influenced by defects and voids at the nanoscale. Pre-2026, there is a need for precise, molecular-level control over cavity formation to tailor properties like charge transport and light absorption for applications in photovoltaics and quantum materials. Existing templating methods often lack the atomic precision and dynamic programmability required for creating well-defined, functional cavities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that self-assembling peptide amphiphile (PA) nanostructures can act as dynamic, sacrificial templates to create specific, size- and shape-controlled cavities within hybrid organic-inorganic semiconductor matrices, and that these cavities will significantly modulate the material's electronic density of states and charge carrier dynamics.
proposals.method: We will design a library of PAs with β-sheet-forming peptide segments and semiconducting polymer-conjugated hydrophobic tails. These will co-assemble into nanofibers or micelles with defined core-shell architectures. These supramolecular structures will be used as templates during the sol-gel synthesis of inorganic semiconductors (e.g., ZnO, TiO2). Subsequent removal of the organic template via mild calcination or solvent extraction will leave behind cavities templated by the PA nanostructure. Electronic properties will be characterized by UV-Vis spectroscopy, photoluminescence, and conductive atomic force microscopy, correlating cavity morphology (from TEM and SAXS) with function.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new bio-inspired paradigm for 'cavity engineering' in semiconductors, moving beyond doping or composite formation. It will provide a foundational understanding of how precisely defined nanoscale voids influence charge trapping and exciton dynamics. The methodology will deliver a versatile toolkit for creating next-generation templated semiconductors with customized optoelectronic profiles.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using dynamic, self-assembling peptide amphiphiles—typically employed in biomedicine—as programmable 'molecular manipulators' to sculpt cavities in hard inorganic semiconductors, a significant departure from static block copolymer or colloidal crystal templating. This approach merges supramolecular design with materials synthesis to achieve unprecedented cavity precision.

proposals.title: Supramolecular Peptide Amphiphile Templates for the Bioinspired Synthesis and Shaping of Hierarchical Ceramics
proposals.motivation: Advanced ceramics require precise control over microstructure and shape for applications in energy, medicine, and catalysis. Traditional processing often relies on high temperatures and pressures, limiting complexity and compatibility with organic components. Inspired by biomineralization, where organic matrices direct inorganic growth, there is a major opportunity to develop mild, solution-based ceramic processing guided by designed supramolecular assemblies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that peptide amphiphile (PA) supramolecular nanostructures, with precisely tuned surface chemistry and nanoscale morphology, can act as dynamic organic manipulators to template the nucleation, growth, and macroscopic shaping of ceramic phases (e.g., silica, hydroxyapatite, titania) under ambient conditions.
proposals.method: We will design a library of PAs with peptide sequences selected to bind specific ceramic precursors (e.g., silicate, phosphate, or titania ions) and self-assemble into high-aspect-ratio nanofibers or nanotubes. Using established methods from the Stupp lab, we will control co-assembly and self-sorting of multiple PAs to create hybrid organic-inorganic gels. Ceramic precursors will be introduced into these dynamic matrices, where the supramolecular scaffolds will direct mineralization. We will employ techniques like solvent casting, 3D printing of PA inks, and electrospinning to shape the hybrid gels prior to mineralization, followed by mild calcination or chemical crosslinking to produce the final ceramic object.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for fabricating ceramics with controlled porosity, toughness, and complex shapes without requiring extreme sintering. It will demonstrate how supramolecular design can translate molecular information into macroscopic material properties in inorganic systems. The resulting hybrid protocols could enable next-generation bioceramics for bone regeneration or structured catalysts.
proposals.novelty: While PA templating of hydroxyapatite is known, extending this to a broad range of ceramics and integrating it with advanced shaping techniques like 3D printing is unexplored. The novelty lies in using the dynamic, multicomponent self-assembly expertise of the Stupp lab to create *processable* organic templates that actively guide ceramic formation and final geometry.

proposals.title: De Novo Protein Design of β-Sheet Nanoribbons for the Nucleation and Morphological Control of Inorganic Phases
proposals.motivation: The controlled nucleation and growth of inorganic materials by biological templates is a powerful route to hybrid materials, but it is largely limited to known, naturally occurring proteins. This restricts the diversity of mineral phases, crystal habits, and material properties that can be accessed. A rational, computational approach to design entirely new protein scaffolds for inorganic nucleation would unlock unprecedented control over hybrid material synthesis, enabling applications in catalysis, sensing, and energy storage.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that computationally designed de novo proteins, featuring stable β-sheet folds and precisely positioned charged/polar residues, can act as programmable nucleation sites for specific non-biological inorganic phases (e.g., metal oxides, sulfides), dictating crystal phase, orientation, and nanostructure morphology.
proposals.method: We will employ the Rosetta protein design suite to design short peptide sequences that autonomously fold into stable, parallel β-sheet nanoribbons, inspired by Stupp's peptide amphiphile work. Using molecular dynamics simulations (GROMACS) to assess stability, we will computationally screen designs for their interaction energies with target inorganic surfaces (e.g., ZnO, CdS) via docking and free energy calculations. Top candidates will be synthesized via solid-phase peptide synthesis, self-assembled into nanostructures, and exposed to precursor solutions. Nucleation will be characterized using TEM, SAED, and XRD.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general computational framework for designing proteinaceous nucleants for abiotic minerals, moving beyond biomimetic replication. It will yield a new class of hybrid materials where the inorganic component's phase and nanoarchitecture are dictated by a synthetically programmable organic matrix, bridging supramolecular chemistry and materials science.
proposals.novelty: While peptide amphiphiles and known biomineralization proteins have been used to template minerals, the de novo computational design of a minimal, fold-defined protein scaffold specifically to nucleate and control non-biological inorganic phases is a novel and untested paradigm for creating hybrid materials.

proposals.title: Inorganic Microstructure-Templated Supramolecular Polymerization of Peptide Amphiphiles for Hybrid Biomaterials
proposals.motivation: The integration of organic and inorganic components is crucial for creating advanced biomaterials that mimic the complex, hierarchical structure of native tissues like bone and cartilage. Pre-2026, supramolecular polymers, particularly peptide amphiphiles (PAs), offer exquisite bioactivity and dynamic self-assembly, but controlling their long-range order and macroscopic morphology within composite materials remains a challenge. Using pre-formed inorganic phases as templates could provide a powerful route to dictate the spatial organization and emergent properties of these bioactive organic matrices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the surface chemistry, topography, and ionic microenvironment of pre-formed inorganic microstructures (e.g., calcium phosphate spherulites, silica networks) can direct the nucleation, growth, and final supramolecular morphology of co-assembling peptide amphiphile and polymer systems, leading to hybrid materials with non-native, yet precisely controlled, hierarchical architectures.
proposals.method: We will synthesize anionic calcium phosphate or silica microstructures with controlled shapes (rods, plates, porous networks) via established precipitation methods. These will be incubated in aqueous solutions containing mixtures of known bioactive PAs (e.g., a chondrogenic TGF-β1 mimetic cyclic PA) and complementary, charged polymers. Using cryo-electron tomography, super-resolution microscopy, and synchrotron X-ray scattering, we will map the spatial relationship and orientation of the resulting PA nanofibers relative to the inorganic template. We will systematically vary template surface charge, polymer chemistry, and ionic strength to establish design rules for directed assembly.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for 'directed supramolecular polymerization' using inorganic templates, moving beyond self-assembly in bulk solution. It will yield a foundational understanding of how to engineer the mesoscale architecture of bioactive supramolecular materials. The resulting hybrid systems could lead to improved scaffolds for regenerative medicine where inorganic phase organization dictates the presentation of biological signals.
proposals.novelty: While Stupp's prior work has explored co-assembly of PAs with polymers or their presentation on cell surfaces, using pre-formed inorganic microstructures as a *morphological template* for the supramolecular polymerization process itself is a novel and non-obvious extension. This approach inverts the typical composite strategy, letting the inorganic phase guide the organic assembly rather than merely being a passive filler.

proposals.title: Cooperative Supramolecular Manipulators for Hierarchical Control of Bioactive Material Microstructure
proposals.motivation: A major challenge in biomaterials science is the precise, multi-scale organization of bioactive signals to direct complex cellular processes like tissue morphogenesis. While supramolecular chemistry enables nanoscale control, bridging this to the microscale and macroscale of functional tissues remains an unsolved problem. Achieving hierarchical control would unlock new regenerative therapies and advanced cell culture platforms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing a system of cooperative peptide amphiphile (PA) molecular manipulators with orthogonal self-assembly triggers, we can program the sequential and spatial organization of nanostructures into higher-order micro- and macro-architectures with defined bioactivity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of PAs with distinct bioactive epitopes (e.g., TGF-β1 mimetic cyclic peptides, laminin-derived sequences) and tunable assembly domains responsive to specific cues (pH, enzymes, light). Using principles from recent work on self-sorting vs. co-assembly, we will design cooperative pairs where one PA acts as a 'nucleator' and another as a 'propagator'. High-resolution microscopy (cryo-TEM, SEM) and spectroscopic techniques will characterize the hierarchical structures. Cell-based assays with primary chondrocytes and mesenchymal stem cells will test the bioactivity of the resulting multi-scale matrices.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for supramolecular biomaterials, moving from passive self-assembly to active, cooperative molecular manipulation. It will provide a concrete methodology to engineer materials with integrated bioactivity across nano, micro, and macro scales, directly impacting tissue engineering and fundamental studies of cell-matrix interactions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit design of *cooperative* molecular manipulators, rather than single-component systems, to achieve programmable hierarchical control. This approach integrates orthogonal assembly logic with bioactive signaling in a way not yet demonstrated in pre-2026 supramolecular biomaterials.

proposals.title: Programming Peptide Amphiphile Self-Assembly in Non-Aqueous and Extreme pH Solvents for Tunable Nanostructure and Function
proposals.motivation: Peptide amphiphile (PA) self-assembly is almost exclusively studied in aqueous, near-physiological conditions, limiting the exploration of nanostructure morphologies and material properties accessible only in non-native environments. Pre-2026 work from the Stupp lab and others has demonstrated the profound bioactivity of PA assemblies but has not systematically decoupled assembly pathways from biological function to discover new materials. Exploring assembly in organic solvents, ionic liquids, and extreme pH could reveal fundamental design rules for supramolecular polymers beyond biomimicry, enabling applications in catalysis, optoelectronics, or separations where aqueous stability is a limitation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically varying solvent polarity, dielectric constant, and pH far from physiological norms, we can access unique, kinetically trapped, or thermodynamically stable PA nanostructures with emergent optical, mechanical, or catalytic properties not possible in water.
proposals.method: We will design a library of PAs with known aqueous assembly motifs (e.g., β-sheet formers, cylindrical micelle builders) and probe their self-assembly in solvents like hexafluoroisopropanol, DMSO, and glycerol, as well as in aqueous buffers at pH <3 and >11. Assembly will be characterized using cryo-TEM, synchrotron SAXS, and circular dichroism. We will employ solvent exchange and thermal annealing protocols to map energy landscapes. Key to the approach is correlating solvent parameters (Kamlet-Taft, dielectric) with final nanostructure using multivariate analysis, and functional screening for properties like charge transport (via conductive AFM) or organocatalysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first comprehensive framework for predicting PA assembly outcomes in non-aqueous solvents, expanding the supramolecular materials design space. It will contribute new, stable nanostructured materials with potential for energy-relevant or industrial applications, and provide fundamental insights into the competition between hydrogen bonding, solvophobic, and electrostatic interactions in extreme environments.
proposals.novelty: While solvent-switch assembly is known, a systematic exploration of PA nanostructure and function across a wide continuum of non-physiological solvents and pH is unprecedented. The novelty lies in deliberately divorcing the bio-inspired assembly process from its native aqueous context to discover entirely new material properties, moving beyond the dominant paradigm of biomedical application.

proposals.title: Bioactive Tails in Supramolecular Nanofibers: A Hydrophobic Domain-Driven Strategy for Controlling Stem Cell Fate
proposals.motivation: In peptide amphiphile (PA) design, the canonical paradigm treats the peptide segment as the exclusive bioactive signal, with the hydrophobic tail serving merely as a structural driver for self-assembly. This limits the functional density and chemical diversity of supramolecular biomaterials. By inverting this assumption, we can create a new class of PAs where bioactivity is an emergent property of the supramolecular nanostructure, programmed by the chemical functionality of the tail, potentially unlocking simpler, more stable, and more potent materials for regenerative medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing hydrophobic tails with specific chemical functionalities (e.g., enzyme-mimetic groups, lipid-like signaling moieties, or synthetic drug conjugates), the resulting supramolecular nanofibers will present a bioactive surface that can directly and potently influence cellular processes, independent of a canonical peptide epitope.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of PAs featuring a constant, inert beta-sheet-forming peptide sequence (e.g., VVVAAA) coupled to diverse hydrophobic tails. Tails will be systematically varied to include: 1) steroid derivatives (e.g., cholic acid) for membrane interaction, 2) synthetic phospholipid headgroups for signaling, and 3) conjugated small molecules (e.g., retinoic acid). Self-assembly into nanofibers will be characterized by cryo-TEM and CD spectroscopy. Bioactivity will be assessed by culturing human mesenchymal stem cells on nanofiber coatings, quantifying differentiation markers (osteogenic, chondrogenic, adipogenic) via qPCR and immunostaining, and comparing to traditional peptide-signaling PAs.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational design principle for 'tail-first' bioactive supramolecular materials, expanding the functional palette beyond amino acids. It will demonstrate that complex cell signaling can be engineered through the hydrophobic domain, leading to simpler, more proteolytically stable, and cost-effective biomimetic materials. This could redefine the minimal structural requirements for bioactive nanostructures in tissue engineering.
