proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the entrenched dogma that bioactivity in PAs must be encoded in the peptide sequence. The novelty lies in deliberately assigning the primary signaling role to the historically 'passive' hydrophobic tail, creating a new axis for material functionality that bypasses peptide synthesis constraints.

proposals.title: Supramolecular Design Rules for Steering Peptide Amphiphile Self-Assembly into 2D Sheets and 0D Micelles
proposals.motivation: Peptide amphiphile (PA) research has been dominated by the one-dimensional nanofiber geometry, driven by the robust β-sheet-driven cylindrical micelle paradigm. This focus has limited the exploration of other morphologies with distinct biointerfacial properties. Pre-2026 work on self-sorting and coassembly suggests that geometric control beyond fibers is possible but underexplored, and such control could unlock new material functions in drug delivery, membrane mimicry, and 2D cell culture scaffolds.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically manipulating PA molecular architecture—specifically the steric bulk of the hydrophobic segment, the lateral cross-sectional area of the peptide headgroup, and the strategic placement of charged residues—we can suppress 1D elongation and exclusively favor the thermodynamic formation of either 2D bilayer sheets or 0D spherical micelles.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a library of PAs with variations in: 1) alkyl tail branching (e.g., phytanyl) and double-tail geometries to increase hydrophobic volume and frustrate cylindrical packing; 2) cyclic or branched peptide sequences (inspired by TGF-β1 mimetics) to enforce a large, rigid cross-sectional area promoting 2D extension; and 3) charged residues placed to create intramolecular electrostatic repulsion within the corona, limiting fiber growth. Assembly will be characterized by cryo-TEM, SAXS, and CD spectroscopy, with morphology outcomes correlated to molecular parameters using principal component analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a concrete design framework for achieving non-fibrillar PA nanostructures, moving beyond empirical discovery. It will contribute new, well-defined 2D and 0D supramolecular biomaterials to the toolkit for nanotechnology, enabling direct testing of how nanoscale shape influences biological signaling and material mechanics in regenerative medicine.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate de-prioritization of the nanofiber as the target structure, a radical shift in the field, and the systematic application of steric and electrostatic frustration principles—concepts from polymer and surfactant science—to achieve exclusive morphological control in peptide amphiphile systems.

proposals.title: Supramolecular Theranostic Nanostructures: Co-assembling Peptide Amphiphiles with Imaging Moieties for Real-Time Monitoring and Treatment of Fibrosis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, peptide amphiphile (PA) nanostructures have shown immense promise in regenerative medicine and targeted drug delivery, but integrating real-time, non-invasive imaging capabilities remains a significant challenge. This limits the ability to monitor therapeutic localization, biodistribution, and efficacy in vivo. Developing a single, self-assembling material that combines a therapeutic function with a robust diagnostic signal would enable precision theranostics, particularly for dynamic pathologies like tissue fibrosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-assembling therapeutic PAs with specifically designed, non-perturbing imaging-agent-conjugated PAs, we can create stable, injectable supramolecular nanofibers that simultaneously deliver a bioactive signal (e.g., a TGF-β mimetic cyclic peptide) and provide a high-contrast magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) signal via incorporated gadolinium chelates for real-time treatment monitoring.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of PAs: (1) a bioactive PA displaying the cyclic TGF-β1 mimetic peptide; (2) a diagnostic PA with a DOTA-Gd chelate linked via a hydrophilic spacer to minimize disruption of self-assembly and relaxivity quenching. Using established methods from the Stupp lab, we will employ pH-triggered self-assembly and systematically study the co-assembly vs. self-sorting behavior of these components using cryo-TEM, CD spectroscopy, and SAXS. The nanostructures will be tested in vitro for chondrogenic bioactivity and relaxivity, and subsequently in a murine model of pulmonary fibrosis using T1-weighted MRI to track localization and correlate with histological assessment of therapeutic outcome.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable supramolecular design principle for creating integrated theranostic biomaterials. It will provide a foundational platform for real-time, non-invasive monitoring of supramolecular therapeutic delivery and performance in vivo. The project will yield critical structure-function insights into how imaging moieties can be incorporated into dynamic self-assembled systems without compromising their bioactivity or nanostructure.
proposals.novelty: While PAs have been used for therapy and imaging separately, the novelty lies in the deliberate, co-assembled integration of a high-payload MRI agent within a bioactive nanostructure, leveraging the lab's expertise in controlling self-sorting vs. co-assembly to create a single, multifunctional material rather than a simple mixture of two components.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Supramolecular-Biopolymer Composite Scaffolds for Spatially Programmed Bone Regeneration
proposals.motivation: While peptide amphiphile (PA) nanofibers provide excellent nanoscale bioactivity and cell signaling, their mechanical properties and structural hierarchy are limited for regenerating complex tissues like bone, which requires integrated toughness, porosity, and osteogenic cues. Pre-2026 work in the Stupp lab and others has shown the power of PA supramolecular assemblies but has not fully integrated them with established, processable biomaterials to create multi-scale, mechanically robust constructs that mimic the native extracellular matrix's hierarchical organization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the co-assembly of bioactive PA nanofibers within a mechanically supportive, macroporous polymer matrix (e.g., polycaprolactone or gelatin methacryloyl) will create a hierarchical composite that synergistically combines the nanoscale bioactivity of supramolecular signaling with the tunable macroscale mechanics and architecture of polymers, leading to enhanced osteogenic differentiation and mineralized tissue formation.
proposals.method: We will design anionic or cationic PAs bearing the RGDS and a phosphoserine motif for cell adhesion and biomineralization. These PAs will be co-assembled into nanofibers and then integrated into a macroporous polymer scaffold using a combination of electrospinning (for polymer fibers) and infusion/thermal gelation of the PA solution. We will employ cryo-electron microscopy and small-angle X-ray scattering to characterize the preservation of PA nanostructure within the composite. The bioactivity will be tested using human mesenchymal stem cells, assessing spatially controlled differentiation via immunostaining for osteogenic markers and micro-CT for mineral deposition.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general methodology for creating hierarchically ordered supramolecular-polymer composites, moving beyond simple blending to controlled integration. It will demonstrate how to spatially encode bioactivity within a mechanically functional scaffold, providing a new platform for regenerating tissues that require both structural support and complex biological signaling.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate, multi-scale integration where the PA nanofibers are not merely additives but form a bioactive nanofiber network within the pores of a fabricated polymer scaffold, creating two distinct, interpenetrating levels of architectural control that are independently tunable—a significant advance over homogeneous composites or surface coatings.

proposals.title: Charge as a Design Parameter for Macroscopic Alignment in Supramolecular Peptide Amphiphile Assemblies
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work from the Stupp lab has demonstrated that charged peptide amphiphiles (PAs) can self-assemble into nanostructures that subsequently align into macroscopic, bioactive materials, such as aligned nanofiber matrices for cell culture. The role of electrostatic interactions in driving the initial self-assembly and the subsequent pathway to long-range order is not fully understood. Determining if this pathway is unique to charged systems or accessible to neutral amphiphiles is crucial for expanding the design space of supramolecular biomaterials, particularly for applications where electrostatic interactions with cells or proteins must be minimized.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the electrostatic repulsion between charged headgroups in peptide amphiphiles is a critical factor in directing the kinetic pathway of self-assembly that leads to macroscopic alignment, and that purely neutral amphiphiles will fail to access this same pathway under identical conditions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of peptide amphiphiles with systematically varied headgroup charges (anionic, cationic, zwitterionic, and neutral) but identical hydrophobic tails and β-sheet-forming peptide segments. Using established methods from the lab, we will induce self-assembly via pH or ionic strength triggers and monitor nanostructure formation using cryo-TEM and SAXS. Macroscopic alignment will be achieved using mechanical shear flow in a controlled geometry, with alignment quantified by polarized optical microscopy and SAXS. The coassembly and self-sorting behavior of charged and neutral PAs will be investigated using fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) pairs to probe mixing at the molecular level.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental design rule connecting molecular charge to the hierarchical assembly of supramolecular materials. It will clarify whether macroscopic alignment is a general property of supramolecular polymers or a specific outcome of electrostatic tuning. The findings will directly inform the design of next-generation biomaterials for tissue engineering where control over matrix architecture and bio-inertness is required.
proposals.novelty: While the role of charge in PA self-assembly is known, its specific necessity for the kinetic pathway leading to shear-induced macroscopic alignment has not been systematically isolated and tested. This study moves beyond describing nanostructure to explicitly probing the pathway dependence of a critical material property.

proposals.title: Supramolecularly Patterned Nanofiber Gels for Single-Cell Mechanotransduction and Polarity Studies
proposals.motivation: Understanding how extracellular matrix (ECM) architecture directs single-cell polarity and fate is a fundamental challenge in cell biology and regenerative medicine. Pre-2026 techniques often average signals across cell populations, obscuring individual cellular responses to precise nanoscale cues. A platform enabling deterministic control over the alignment and polarity of isolated cells would provide unprecedented insight into mechanotransduction and signaling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that micro-patterned, anisotropic gels composed of peptide amphiphile (PA) nanofibers can be engineered to impose defined alignment and polarity on individual adherent cells, and that this controlled microenvironment will direct specific cytoskeletal organization and downstream signaling pathways in a predictable manner.
proposals.method: We will create micropatterned substrates using two-photon laser scanning lithography or soft lithography to define adhesive islands. On these islands, we will co-assemble mixtures of bioactive (e.g., IKVAV, RGD-presenting) and inert PAs to form aligned supramolecular nanofiber gels, leveraging Stupp lab's expertise in controlling self-sorting vs. coassembly. High-resolution live-cell imaging (e.g., confocal, TIRF) will be used to track single-cell responses, including cytoskeletal dynamics (via LifeAct-GFP) and nucleus translocation, to the engineered anisotropic cues.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel synthetic biology tool for single-cell analysis of ECM-guided polarity. It will directly link nanoscale supramolecular order to single-cell phenotypic outputs, providing foundational knowledge for designing next-generation biomaterials that instruct cell behavior with single-cell precision for applications in neural repair or engineered tissue constructs.
proposals.novelty: While PA nanofibers are known to guide cell populations, the deliberate micropatterning of their alignment at the single-cell scale to interrogate polarity is unexplored. The approach uniquely combines supramolecular design of the nanofiber matrix with microfabrication to create isolated, architecturally defined micro-niches.

proposals.title: Evolutionary Selection of Supramolecular Nanofibers from Complex Peptide Amphiphile Libraries
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular biomaterials, such as peptide amphiphile (PA) nanofibers, are typically constructed from meticulously pre-designed molecular units to ensure precise assembly and function. This design-intensive approach limits the exploration of vast chemical space and may overlook emergent, functionally superior nanostructures that arise from complex mixtures. Inspired by evolutionary principles in biology and pre-2026 work on dynamic combinatorial libraries, this proposal challenges the necessity of pure, pre-designed building blocks by asking if functional 1D nanostructures can be discovered through in vitro selection pressures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that iterative cycles of assembly and selection, applied to a complex library of semi-randomized peptide amphiphiles, will drive the evolution of specific molecular sub-populations capable of forming stable, bioactive 1D nanostructures, without prior knowledge of their precise sequence.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a one-bead-one-compound library of PAs featuring a constant alkyl tail and a variable peptide headgroup region with defined positions for randomization. This complex mixture will be subjected to iterative selection cycles: (1) induction of self-assembly under controlled conditions (pH, temperature), (2) physical isolation of formed nanofibers via centrifugation or filtration, (3) characterization of selected nanostructures by cryo-TEM and CD spectroscopy, and (4) amplification of the molecular constituents within the selected fibers via PCR (for DNA-barcoded PAs) or re-synthesis based on mass spectrometry sequencing. Selection pressures will include thermodynamic stability, mechanical rigidity, and specific bioactivity (e.g., binding to a target protein).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept platform for the 'directed evolution' of supramolecular nanomaterials, moving the field from a purely rational design paradigm to one that incorporates combinatorial discovery. It could reveal new, non-intuitive structure-function relationships in peptide amphiphiles and generate nanostructures with optimized properties for catalysis or cell signaling. The methodology would provide a general route to explore complex, hybrid molecular systems for emergent functions.
proposals.novelty: While dynamic combinatorial chemistry and peptide libraries exist, applying iterative, Darwinian selection pressures to evolve functional supramolecular nanostructures from non-predesigned mixtures in a materials context is a novel and non-obvious extension of Stupp's work on PA co-assembly and bioactivity.

proposals.title: Living Supramolecular Polymerization of Peptide Amphiphiles for Monodisperse Nanofibers with Tunable Length
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular nanostructures, particularly one-dimensional nanofibers from peptide amphiphiles (PAs), are powerful bioactive materials for regenerative medicine and nanotechnology. However, their polydisperse nature, arising from uncontrolled nucleation and growth, limits precise structure-function studies and reproducible therapeutic outcomes. Achieving monodisperse, length-defined supramolecular polymers remains a fundamental challenge in the field, hindering the translation of these materials into standardized technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a seeded, living supramolecular polymerization mechanism can be established for specific PA systems, enabling precise control over nanofiber length and polydispersity by separating the nucleation and elongation phases.
proposals.method: We will design a library of PAs with systematically varied β-sheet-forming peptide segments and hydrophobic tails to identify candidates exhibiting cooperative, nucleation-elongation kinetics. Using a high-throughput microfluidic platform, we will initiate polymerization with a controlled number of sonication-generated seed nuclei. Growth will be monitored in real-time via Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) pairs and stopped at predetermined points by introducing a chain-capping monomer or by rapid thermal quenching. Length and uniformity will be characterized by cryo-TEM and analytical ultracentrifugation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first generalizable methodology for producing monodisperse supramolecular peptide nanofibers with programmable lengths. It will provide a foundational platform for correlating nanofiber dimensions with biological activity (e.g., cell signaling, matrix mechanics) and for constructing hybrid materials with precise hierarchical order.
proposals.novelty: While living polymerization is well-known in covalent polymers, achieving it in aqueous, biologically relevant supramolecular systems is non-trivial. This approach moves beyond empirical mixing or environmental control to an active, kinetic programming of nanostructure assembly.

proposals.title: Programmable 1D Supramolecular Polymers via DNA-Encoded Recognition in Synthetic Peptide Amphiphiles
proposals.motivation: DNA origami demonstrates exquisite control over nanostructure assembly via programmable base-pairing, but its components are limited to nucleic acids. Extending this programmability to synthetic, non-biological molecules like peptide amphiphiles (PAs) would unlock new classes of functional, bioinspired materials with tunable properties. This addresses a key challenge in supramolecular chemistry: achieving predictable, long-range order in 1D assemblies of synthetic molecules without relying on biological folding.
proposals.hypothesis: That synthetic peptide amphiphiles functionalized with short, single-stranded DNA 'barcodes' will undergo predictable, programmable 1D co-assembly or self-sorting based on Watson-Crick complementarity, directing nanostructure morphology and hierarchical order.
proposals.method: We will design a library of PAs bearing hydrophobic alkyl tails and peptide segments (e.g., β-sheet formers) linked to short (5-10 nt) DNA sequences. Using established solid-phase synthesis, we will create PA pairs with complementary and non-complementary DNA handles. Assembly will be triggered in aqueous solution, and nanostructure formation will be characterized by cryo-TEM, SAXS, and FRET. The sequence-specificity of assembly will be tested by mixing multiple PA types and analyzing co-assembly vs. self-sorting via chromatography and fluorescence assays with DNA-intercalating dyes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable platform for encoding assembly instructions into synthetic molecules, bridging DNA nanotechnology and supramolecular polymer science. It will yield design rules for creating hybrid biomolecular-synthetic materials with predictable architectures, advancing towards applications in controlled drug delivery and bioactive scaffolds where precise nanoscale presentation of signals is critical.
proposals.novelty: While DNA-PA conjugates exist, applying them not merely as tags but as programmable 'directors' of 1D supramolecular polymerization to control length, composition, and hierarchy is a novel strategy. This moves beyond using DNA for static attachment to using it for dynamic, information-driven assembly of non-nucleic acid building blocks.

proposals.title: Continuous Flow Assembly of Macroscopic, Hierarchical Fibers from Supramolecular Peptide Amphiphile Nanofilaments
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular peptide amphiphiles (PAs) self-assemble into bioactive 1D nanostructures with exceptional control over cell signaling, but their application in tissue engineering and regenerative medicine is limited by their form as injectable gels or coatings. A scalable method to produce macroscopic, aligned fibrous materials from these molecular assemblies would bridge the gap between nanoscale bioactivity and macroscale structural integrity, enabling new classes of biomimetic scaffolds for tendon, ligament, or neural repair. Pre-2026 work has demonstrated bioactivity and self-sorting in PA systems, but not their integration into continuous, processable macroscopic threads.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a continuous flow-focusing or microfluidic spinning process can direct the hierarchical assembly of peptide amphiphile nanofilaments into aligned, cohesive macroscopic fibers, preserving their bioactivity while imparting tunable mechanical properties.
proposals.method: We will design a coaxial flow-focusing device where a core stream of PA solution (e.g., a TGF-β mimetic or self-sorting PA mixture) is injected into a sheath stream containing ions or polymers to trigger supramolecular assembly. Process parameters (flow rates, pH, ionic strength) will be optimized to align nanofilaments via shear and interfacial forces, with continuous fiber collection on a rotating mandrel. We will integrate real-time characterization using micro-rheology and polarized optical microscopy to monitor assembly dynamics. The mechanical properties, nanostructure (via cryo-TEM), and bioactivity (via chondrogenic differentiation assays) of the resulting fibers will be characterized.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational platform for the continuous manufacturing of macroscopic materials from supramolecular building blocks, directly translating molecular biofunctionality to the macro-scale. It will provide new insights into the non-equilibrium dynamics of hierarchical self-assembly under flow. The resulting fibers will constitute a novel biomaterial format for load-bearing biomedical applications where nanoscale signaling and macroscopic alignment are both critical.
proposals.novelty: While electrospinning of polymers is well-known, the continuous, directed assembly of purely supramolecular, bioactive filaments into macroscopic fibers is unprecedented. The approach uniquely leverages the dynamic, non-covalent nature of PA assemblies within a flow process, a significant departure from processing stable polymers or hybrid composites.

proposals.title: Controlled Disassembly of Peptide Amphiphile Nanofibers: A Kinetic and Thermodynamic Framework for Monomer Recovery and Material Reprogramming
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular assembly of peptide amphiphiles (PAs) into 1D nanostructures is well-studied for creating bioactive materials. However, the controlled, non-destructive disassembly of these nanostructures back to functional monomers remains a critical, unsolved challenge. Understanding and controlling this inverse process is essential for developing recyclable biomaterials, enabling on-demand therapeutic release, and creating dynamic systems that can be reprogrammed, which is largely unexplored in pre-2026 literature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the disassembly kinetics and thermodynamics of PA nanofibers can be systematically controlled by engineering specific molecular triggers—such as competitive binders, enzymatic cleavage sites, and pH/redox switches—leading to predictable monomer recovery and enabling the recycling or reprogramming of supramolecular materials.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of PAs with known assembly motifs (e.g., β-sheet formers, charged segments) and incorporate cleavable linkers (enzyme-sensitive, redox-active) or competitive binding epitopes. Disassembly will be triggered using specific enzymes (e.g., matrix metalloproteinases), redox agents (e.g., glutathione), or competitive small molecules. We will monitor the process in real-time using high-resolution techniques: time-resolved small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) for nanostructural changes, fluorescence correlation spectroscopy (FCS) for monomer diffusion, and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) to quantify recovered monomers and their integrity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative framework linking molecular design to disassembly pathways and kinetics for supramolecular biomaterials. It will provide fundamental principles for creating recyclable or transient nanomaterials and open new avenues for therapeutic delivery systems where complete carrier disappearance is required.
proposals.novelty: While extensive work focuses on assembly, a systematic study of the 'inverse problem' of controlled disassembly to monomers is novel. The approach integrates Stupp's expertise in PA design with real-time, multi-scale analytics to map disassembly mechanisms, moving beyond simple degradation to controlled monomer recovery.

proposals.title: Emergent Supramolecular Order from Minimalist Peptide Amphiphile Libraries via High-Throughput Screening and Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular design has relied on pre-encoded molecular recognition motifs, limiting discovery to predictable interactions. Exploiting emergent, non-predicted assembly from simple components could unlock new bioactive materials and complex functions not accessible through rational design. This aligns with the growing pre-2026 interest in complex systems and high-throughput exploration in materials science.
proposals.hypothesis: That high-throughput screening of combinatorially mixed, minimalist peptide amphiphile (PA) libraries will reveal unexpected, synergistic co-assemblies with emergent nanostructures and bioactivities not present in the individual components.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of minimalist PAs varying only in headgroup charge (Lys, Glu, uncharged) and alkyl tail length. Using an automated liquid handling platform, we will create thousands of binary and ternary mixtures across a full composition space. Each mixture will be characterized for nanostructure (cryo-TEM, SAXS) and selected for bioactivity (e.g., chondrogenic differentiation, inspired by Stupp's TGF-β mimetic work). A machine learning model (e.g., random forest or simple neural network) will be trained on the composition-structure-activity dataset to identify non-linear interaction rules and predict new mixtures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a data-driven methodology for discovering supramolecular materials without pre-defined recognition sites, moving the field from rational design to exploratory discovery. It will produce a set of design rules for emergent co-assembly and likely identify novel bioactive matrices for cell culture and tissue engineering.
proposals.novelty: While self-sorting and co-assembly are known, systematically mining the vast, under-explored compositional space of simple PA mixtures for *unpredictable* synergistic outcomes using high-throughput experimentation and ML is a novel paradigm shift for the supramolecular field.

proposals.title: Continuous Extrusion of Bioactive Supramolecular Peptide-Polymer Hybrid Fibers for Scalable Tissue Engineering
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular peptide amphiphile (PA) materials developed by the Stupp lab exhibit remarkable bioactivity, such as chondrogenic induction and single-cell coating, but are produced via batch methods like solvent casting or manual injection. This severely limits the quantity, reproducibility, and geometric control needed for clinical translation and industrial applications in regenerative medicine. A scalable, continuous manufacturing platform is a critical, unmet technological gap for these dynamic, non-covalent polymers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dynamic self-assembly of peptide amphiphiles and their co-assemblies with synthetic polymers can be engineered to occur under the non-equilibrium, shear-driven conditions of a continuous extrusion or roll-to-roll process, yielding macroscopic materials with preserved nanoscale architecture and bioactivity.
proposals.method: We will design a modular twin-screw extrusion line with integrated in-line monitoring (rheometry, SAXS/WAXS). The process will be developed using a model bioactive PA (e.g., a TGF-β1 mimetic cyclic peptide amphiphile) and a co-assembling biocompatible polymer (e.g., PEG-PLA). Key parameters include controlled solvent exchange zones, temperature gradients, and shear rates to guide hierarchical assembly from solution to gel-state filaments. A secondary roll-to-roll module will be implemented for coating these extrudates onto biodegradable meshes, creating hybrid scaffolds.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first continuous manufacturing platform for supramolecular biomaterials, directly translating gram-scale laboratory discoveries to kilogram-scale production. It will provide a foundational engineering framework for controlling supramolecular order in a flow process and generate prototype scalable scaffolds for cartilage repair, directly addressing a major barrier in the field.
proposals.novelty: While continuous processing is standard for covalent polymers, applying it to dynamically assembling, shear-sensitive supramolecular systems where function is inextricably linked to nanoscale structure is a novel and high-risk engineering challenge. The integration of real-time structural characterization to guide process control for non-covalent materials is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Dynamic Supramolecular Scaffolds for Reconfigurable Hybrid Nanocomposites with Tunable Optoelectronic Properties
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, integrating inorganic nanoparticles (NPs) into functional materials often relies on static covalent linkages, limiting dynamic reconfiguration and responsiveness. Supramolecular chemistry offers reversible interactions, but applying these principles to create complex, dynamically organized NP composites with emergent functions remains a significant challenge. This work addresses the need for next-generation adaptive materials for sensing, catalysis, and photonics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that peptide amphiphile (PA) supramolecular nanostructures, designed with specific binding motifs, can dynamically and reversibly organize inorganic nanoparticles via non-covalent interactions, leading to hybrid composites whose macroscopic properties can be tuned in response to external stimuli.
proposals.method: We will synthesize PAs bearing terminal ligands (e.g., nitrilotriacetic acid for His-tag binding, thiols, or electrostatic patches) known to bind to specific NP surfaces (e.g., Au, quantum dots, magnetic NPs). Co-assembly and self-sorting strategies, informed by Stupp's prior work, will be used to create fibrous or lamellar PA matrices. The dynamic incorporation and release of NPs will be triggered by pH, temperature, or competitive binders, monitored by in-situ scattering and spectroscopy. The optoelectronic properties of the dynamic composites will be characterized.
proposals.contribution: This will establish a general supramolecular strategy for creating reconfigurable organic-inorganic hybrid materials. It will demonstrate how dynamic NP organization, rather than static embedding, can lead to stimuli-responsive optical and electronic behavior. The work will bridge supramolecular materials science with nanoparticle technology, offering new design principles for adaptive composites.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond static NP composites to create dynamically reconfigurable architectures using biologically inspired supramolecular scaffolds, enabling real-time control over NP organization and composite function, which is largely unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Supramolecular Nanostructures with Non-Peptide Hydrogen-Bonding Cores: Exploring Peptidomimetic and Synthetic Oligomer Amphiphiles
proposals.motivation: Peptide amphiphiles (PAs) pioneered by the Stupp lab are powerful for creating bioactive nanostructures, but their peptide cores can be susceptible to proteolytic degradation, limiting in vivo stability and therapeutic potential. Replacing the canonical β-sheet-forming peptide segment with other hydrogen-bonding oligomers could yield nanostructures with enhanced biostability and tunable mechanics while retaining the dynamic, supramolecular character essential for bioactivity. This exploration is critical for advancing supramolecular biomaterials toward clinical translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that bio-polymers (e.g., peptoids, β-peptides) and synthetic oligomers (e.g., ureidopyrimidinone, oligoamides) capable of directional hydrogen bonding can replace peptide segments in amphiphile design to form stable, one-dimensional supramolecular nanostructures, and that the chemical nature of the core will dictate nanostructure morphology, stability, and interactions with cells.
proposals.method: We will design a library of amphiphiles featuring a hydrophobic alkyl tail, a linker, and a core segment of either a peptoid, a β-peptide, or a synthetic oligomer like a ureidopyrimidinone dimer. Self-assembly will be triggered in aqueous solution and characterized by cryo-TEM, SAXS, and FTIR to confirm nanofiber formation and hydrogen-bonding patterns. Coassembly studies with canonical PAs will assess self-sorting behavior. The stability of nanostructures against enzymatic degradation will be quantified, and selected systems will be tested for their ability to display bioactive epitopes and interact with cells in 2D and 3D culture.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for supramolecular biomaterials by moving beyond α-peptide cores, directly addressing stability limitations. It will generate fundamental knowledge on how hydrogen-bonding motifs from non-peptide oligomers govern supramolecular polymerization and nanoscale morphology. The resulting materials could provide more robust scaffolds for regenerative medicine and drug delivery.
proposals.novelty: While non-peptide hydrogen-bonding units are known, their systematic integration as the core structural element in bioactive supramolecular amphiphiles, replacing the entire peptide segment, is unexplored. The novelty lies in creating a new class of 'peptidomimetic amphiphiles' that decouple biofunctionality from proteolytic instability.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Control of Supramolecular Nanofiber Assembly via Orthogonal Enzymatic and Photochemical Triggers in Peptide Amphiphile Hybrids
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work in the Stupp lab has masterfully utilized ionic strength, pH, and simple molecular recognition to trigger peptide amphiphile (PA) self-assembly. However, achieving complex, spatiotemporally controlled assembly within biologically relevant, heterogeneous environments remains a challenge. The development of orthogonal, non-invasive triggers like light and specific enzymes would enable the dynamic patterning of nanostructures with high precision, critical for advanced applications in 4D biomaterials and targeted therapeutic delivery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating enzymatically cleavable peptide sequences and photoisomerizable moieties (e.g., azobenzene) into the molecular design of PAs, we can create hybrid systems that undergo supramolecular fiber assembly only upon the simultaneous or sequential application of two distinct environmental triggers.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of PAs featuring: 1) a matrix metalloproteinase (MMP)-cleavable linker proximal to the hydrophobic tail, and 2) a photoresponsive azobenzene unit within the peptide backbone. Assembly will be characterized by cryo-TEM, CD spectroscopy, and SAXS. We will first test the orthogonality of triggers: MMP-2 enzyme addition alone, or 365 nm light irradiation alone, should not induce bulk assembly. Co-assembly will be triggered only upon enzymatic cleavage followed by light-induced trans-to-cis isomerization, which alters molecular packing. A high-throughput screening platform using fluorescent reporters of assembly will quantify trigger specificity and kinetics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for multi-stimuli-responsive supramolecular materials, moving beyond single-trigger systems. It will provide a foundational design principle for creating 'logic-gated' nanostructures that assemble only in specific microenvironments, such as tumor sites expressing specific enzymes. This directly advances the field of smart biomaterials for regenerative medicine and controlled drug release.
proposals.novelty: While light or enzyme triggers have been explored individually in supramolecular chemistry, their orthogonal integration into a single PA architecture to create a Boolean 'AND' gate for assembly is novel. This approach leverages Stupp's expertise in PA design and co-assembly control to achieve unprecedented spatiotemporal command over nanostructure formation.

proposals.title: Predicting the Mechanical Behavior of Supramolecular Nanofibers: A Polymer Physics Framework for Bioactive Materials Design
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular nanofibers, such as peptide amphiphile assemblies, are central to bioactive materials for regenerative medicine, but their mechanical properties are often empirically tuned. A predictive, quantitative link between molecular structure, nanoscale rigidity, and bulk material mechanics is lacking. Applying established polymer physics theories to these supramolecular polymers would provide a foundational design principle, enabling the rational engineering of materials with tailored stiffness and viscoelasticity for specific cell signaling and tissue integration.
proposals.hypothesis: The mechanical behavior of supramolecular nanofibers, including their persistence length and network elasticity, can be quantitatively predicted by modeling them as semiflexible polymers, with rigidity scaling predictably with core molecular architecture (e.g., β-sheet content, hydrophobic domain size) and intermolecular bonding energy.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of peptide amphiphiles (PAs) with systematically varied β-sheet-forming segments and alkyl tail lengths. Persistence lengths of individual nanofibers will be measured via quantitative analysis of atomic force microscopy (AFM) images using the chain-in-a-box or mean-squared end-to-end distance methods. These experimental data will be fit to the worm-like chain model, with rigidity correlated to molecular parameters. Coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations, parameterized with these results, will be used to predict the shear modulus of PA nanofiber networks using theories for semiflexible polymer gels.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, quantitative scaling relationship between the molecular design of self-assembling peptides and the emergent mechanical properties of their supramolecular nanostructures. It will provide a new computational-experimental framework for *a priori* material design, moving beyond trial-and-error. This will significantly advance the field of bioactive materials by enabling precise mechanical matching to native tissues.
proposals.novelty: While polymer physics theories are mature for covalent polymers, their rigorous application to predict the mechanics of *supramolecular* nanofibers, where non-covalent bonds govern rigidity, is novel. This bridges the gap between supramolecular chemistry's molecular control and soft matter physics' predictive power for biomaterials.

proposals.title: Microfluidic Control of Supramolecular Nanofiber Assembly Kinetics for Tailored Bioactive Matrices
proposals.motivation: The bioactivity of supramolecular peptide amphiphile (PA) nanostructures, such as those mimicking TGF-β1, is critically dependent on their nanoscale architecture and presentation of epitopes, which are governed by assembly kinetics. Current bulk mixing methods yield heterogeneous structures with limited control over fiber length, polydispersity, and epitope density. A platform to precisely control assembly dynamics is needed to establish robust structure-function relationships and engineer reproducible materials for regenerative medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using microfluidic devices to precisely control the mixing of PA solutions with ionic or pH triggers will allow deterministic tuning of nanofiber nucleation and growth kinetics, leading to nanostructures with defined dimensions, internal order, and enhanced bioactivity compared to bulk-assembled counterparts.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate PDMS-based microfluidic mixers (e.g., hydrodynamic flow-focusing or staggered herringbone geometries) to achieve rapid, uniform mixing of PA solutions with assembly triggers like Ca2+ ions or buffer. We will systematically vary flow rates, channel dimensions, and mixing ratios to control shear and diffusion timescales. The resulting nanostructures will be characterized in-line via small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and off-line via cryo-TEM, AFM, and circular dichroism to correlate mixing parameters with fiber morphology, persistence length, and secondary structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational methodology for kinetically controlling supramolecular polymerization, moving the field from empirical formulation to precision engineering. It will directly link processing conditions to nanoscale morphology and the resulting chondrogenic or cell-coating bioactivity of PA matrices, providing a new tool for creating tailored hybrid biomaterials.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics is used for nanoparticle synthesis, its application to control the non-covalent, hierarchical assembly of peptide amphiphiles—a process sensitive to subtle kinetic pathways—is novel and represents a significant convergence of chemical engineering precision with supramolecular materials science.

proposals.title: Electrohydrodynamic Patterning of Peptide Amphiphile Nanofibers for Hybrid Bioelectronic Circuits
proposals.motivation: Integrating bioactive supramolecular nanomaterials with microelectronic devices is a major challenge in creating biohybrid interfaces for sensing and stimulation. Pre-2026 work has shown that peptide amphiphile (PA) nanofibers can direct cell behavior and conduct ions, but their assembly is typically random or globally aligned, lacking the microscopic spatial control needed for circuit integration. A method to direct PA nanofiber assembly into specific, conductive patterns on substrates would enable a new class of biologically integrated devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by combining substrate-based electrohydrodynamic (EHD) confinement with molecular design for controlled self-assembly, we can direct the nucleation and growth of conductive PA nanofibers into predefined microscopic patterns on device-relevant substrates.
proposals.method: We will design PAs with conjugated segments (e.g., oligothiophene) for enhanced conductivity and specific epitopes for bioactivity. Using photolithographically patterned electrodes on silicon or flexible polymer substrates, we will apply AC electric fields in a confined, evaporating PA solution droplet. The EHD flow and dielectrophoretic forces will be tuned to concentrate and align PA monomers at specific pattern sites, triggering localized supramolecular polymerization. We will optimize field frequency, amplitude, and solvent conditions using in-situ optical microscopy and atomic force microscopy to achieve pattern fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel non-lithographic method for patterning functional supramolecular biomaterials at the microscale. It will demonstrate the first direct integration of bioactive, ion-conducting nanofiber circuits onto solid-state substrates, creating a platform for hybrid devices that interface electronics with biological signaling pathways.
proposals.novelty: While EHD patterning is known for polymers and nanoparticles, its application to guide the *supramolecular polymerization* of peptide amphiphiles into stable, patterned nanofiber networks is unexplored. This approach merges top-down field-guided confinement with bottom-up molecular self-assembly in a way that is distinct from simple post-assembly deposition or printing.

proposals.title: Sustained Release of Neurotrophic Factors from Self-Assembling Peptide Amphiphile Nanofiber Matrices for Neural Regeneration
proposals.motivation: Peptide amphiphile (PA) nanofiber matrices are excellent scaffolds for neural tissue engineering, providing bioactive epitopes and structural support. However, the controlled, sustained delivery of soluble neurotrophic factors (e.g., BDNF, GDNF) from these supramolecular systems remains a challenge, limiting their therapeutic efficacy in treating spinal cord injury or neurodegenerative diseases. Integrating established drug-eluting microsphere technology with the dynamic self-assembly of PAs could create a powerful hybrid material for long-term neurotrophic support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that neurotrophic factor-loaded biodegradable polymer microspheres can be incorporated into a self-assembling PA nanofiber matrix, creating a composite material that provides sustained, localized release of bioactive factors while maintaining the nanofiber's structural and bioactive properties.
proposals.method: We will synthesize PLGA microspheres encapsulating BDNF or GDNF using a double-emulsion solvent evaporation method. These microspheres will be co-assembled with a well-characterized neurite-promoting PA (e.g., IKVAV-presenting) by mixing them into the aqueous PA solution prior to triggering self-assembly via charge screening. We will use cryo-TEM and SAXS to characterize composite nanostructure, ELISA to quantify sustained release profiles, and primary neuronal cell cultures to assess bioactivity and neurite outgrowth in vitro.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel method for creating sustained-release supramolecular biomaterials, directly merging polymer-based drug delivery with dynamic peptide self-assembly. It will provide a new platform for neural regeneration strategies that require both topographic guidance and chronic neurotrophic signaling, advancing the field of bioactive materials.
proposals.novelty: While PAs have been used for drug conjugation or non-covalent binding, the physical incorporation of pre-formed, drug-loaded polymer microspheres *during* PA self-assembly to create an intrinsically drug-eluting nanofiber matrix is unexplored. This approach decouples the release kinetics (controlled by the polymer) from the matrix's structural and bioactive properties (controlled by the PA).

ideas.title: Hierarchical Supramolecular Metamaterials for Creep-Resistant, Fully Reversible Integrity
ideas.description: This research designs and fabricates materials where mechanical stability arises not from covalent cross-links but from architecturally guided supramolecular percolation networks. By arranging strong, specific non-covalent motifs (e.g., stacked macrocycles, multivalent metal-coordination clusters) into a hierarchical, stress-redistributing geometry, the system achieves a robust, solid-like response to sustained loads, overcoming the inherent creep and flow of simple associative polymers. The key innovation is the topological design of the network, which ensures that applied stress is dissipated through reversible bond reformation within localized zones, preventing catastrophic failure. This creates a new class of materials with covalent-like integrity under use conditions, yet capable of full, on-demand disassembly.

ideas.title: Programmable co-assembly of non-fibrous units into dynamically anisotropic matrices for oriented mineralization
ideas.description: This redesigned approach investigates if non-fibrous molecular units can be engineered to form dynamically ordered assemblies that direct crystal orientation. The key is to program the building blocks with interactive motifs that induce liquid crystalline order or shear-aligned phases during the mineralization process, creating a transient but pervasive anisotropic environment. This system directly tests if long-range directional control can be decoupled from static fibrous morphology by using dynamic supramolecular order. The outcome will determine if the fundamental requirement is a fibrous shape or simply a spatially anisotropic field, potentially unlocking new design principles for synthetic biomaterials.

ideas.title: Gecko-Inspired Reversible Adhesives: Replacing Covalent Bonds with Hierarchical Non-Covalent Interactions
ideas.description: This idea challenges the necessity of covalent cross-linking for material integrity by drawing an analogy from biomimetic adhesion. In zoology, geckos solved the problem of strong, reversible surface attachment not with permanent chemical bonds, but by evolving a hierarchical foot structure that amplifies weak van der Waals forces. Adapting this solution, the research will explore if synthetic materials with engineered, multi-scale architectures can leverage collective non-covalent interactions (e.g., H-bonding, hydrophobic effects) to provide sufficient mechanical stability, self-healing, and reversible integrity for specific applications, thereby offering an alternative to permanent covalent networks.

ideas.title: Adapting Single-Molecule Biophysics Techniques to Study Mineralization on Isolated Nanofibers
ideas.description: This idea expands the original 'scale down to single-fiber' concept by drawing an analogy from single-molecule biophysics. In that field, complex biological processes are deconstructed by isolating and manipulating individual molecules using tools like optical tweezers and AFM to apply force and observe real-time kinetics. The adapted research idea applies this solution to the problem of studying mineralization initiation. By using a functionalized AFM tip or microfluidic optical trap to physically isolate and hold a single peptide-amphiphile nanofiber, researchers can create a controlled, observable system. Mineralization precursors can then be introduced to this isolated fiber, allowing for direct, real-time observation of where nucleation preferentially occurs on the fiber template, the kinetics of crystal growth, and how the mineralization process mechanically alters the single fiber, providing fundamental insights unobscured by ensemble averaging.

ideas.title: Adapting Photonic Crystal Engineering for DNA-Based Epitope Lattices
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principles of photonic crystal engineering to the problem of precise epitope spacing. In photonics, precise control over the periodic arrangement of dielectric materials is used to manipulate light propagation. The analogous solution involves designing templates that dictate exact lattice constants and defect geometries. Adapted to this research context, DNA origami is used as a programmable 'lattice template' to create two-dimensional crystal-like arrays with atomically precise placement of IKVAV peptides. By designing the DNA scaffold with specific periodicities (e.g., 10nm, 20nm, 50nm spacing) and symmetries (square or hexagonal lattices), or even intentional line defects, we can systematically create defined 'biochemical interaction landscapes'. This approach allows for the systematic investigation of how the nanoscale spatial organization and clustering of epitopes influence neural cell adhesion, receptor activation, and downstream signaling pathways, moving beyond simple spacing to explore the effects of ordered patterns.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Deconvolution of Surface-Directed Hydroxyapatite Nucleation via Combinatorial Nanofiber Libraries and In-Situ Perturbation Analysis
ideas.description: This study moves beyond descriptive kinetics to establish causative mechanisms by employing a combinatorial library of electrospun nanofibers with systematically varied surface chemistries (e.g., specific functional groups, charge density, patterning) and topographies. The nucleation process is actively probed using a suite of synchronized, in-situ techniques—including synchrotron-based scattering and liquid-phase electron microscopy—to collect time-resolved structural and chemical data under controlled solution conditions. By introducing deliberate perturbations to both the solution chemistry and the designed surfaces during mineralization, the research directly tests and quantifies the energetic and structural contributions of specific surface features to nucleation barriers and crystal orientation. This approach transforms the study from passive observation to active mechanistic dissection, enabling the derivation of design principles for predictive biomaterial synthesis.

ideas.title: Electrochemically Guided Mineralization via Ion-Selective Membranes
ideas.description: This idea integrates principles from electrodialysis and water desalination into the original concept of combining self-assembly with electrochemical methods for mineralization. The analogous solution uses ion-selective membranes within an electric field to precisely control and concentrate specific ions. The adapted research idea employs an ion-selective membrane (e.g., a cation-exchange membrane for Ca2+) integrated with a self-assembled organic template. By applying a tailored electrochemical potential, the membrane selectively pumps the target mineral ions directly into the confined volume of the self-assembled structure. This method provides exquisite spatiotemporal control over local ion concentration and supersaturation, synergistically accelerating the templated mineralization process while potentially enabling the formation of complex, hierarchical mineral composites with improved fidelity to the organic template.

Single cells are encapsulated within nanofiber scaffolds that are pre-functionalized with inert bioorthogonal handles. Following encapsulation and arraying, specific epitopes or signaling molecules can be added, removed, or exchanged in real-time using click reactions, allowing precise temporal control over the cellular microenvironment. This enables high-throughput investigation of how dynamic changes in scaffold biochemistry, rather than static presentations, influence cell fate, migration, or drug response across thousands of parallel experiments.

ideas.title: Cryo-ET-Guided Design of Dynamically Cross-Linked Nanofiber Networks
ideas.description: This research direction integrates cryo-electron tomography (cryo-ET) with the development of dynamically cross-linked nanofiber scaffolds. By using cryo-ET to capture high-resolution 3D snapshots of the nanofiber network at different stages of mechanical deformation and cross-linking states, we can directly correlate specific cross-linker chemistries and kinetics with their resulting three-dimensional network architecture and deformation mechanisms. This structural feedback allows for the rational, iterative design of cross-linkers with tailored reversibility and bond lifetimes to achieve prescribed, adaptive mechanical responses, such as strain-stiffening or self-healing. Consequently, the approach moves beyond empirical optimization to a principled design framework where tomographic structural data actively guides the synthesis of next-generation dynamic biomaterials.

ideas.title: Optogenetically controlled neural differentiation within independently tunable 3D physical microenvironments to decouple mechanotransduction from biochemical signaling
ideas.description: This study employs a dual-component system to achieve complete decoupling of biochemical fate cues from physical microenvironmental signals. Neural progenitors are engineered with an optogenetic CRISPR-interference (Opto-CRISPR-i) system to allow precise, light-activated repression of specific genes guiding differentiation. These cells are then cultured in a bio-inert, synthetic hydrogel scaffold where stiffness, fiber alignment, and stress relaxation can be tuned independently. By applying optical inputs and varying scaffold properties in a fully factorial design, the specific contributions and potential synergies of pure mechanotransduction and targeted transcriptional programming on cell fate can be quantified without the confounding coupling inherent to material-bound epitopes.

Real-time in-situ sensors monitor nucleation and growth, providing feedback to dynamically fine-tune the electrical and chemical parameters at the electrode interface. This active control ensures mineralization initiates and propagates selectively within the template's specific binding sites, overcoming spatial randomness. The result is a robust method for producing hierarchically structured mineral composites with precisely controlled morphology and enhanced mechanical properties.

ideas.title: Closed-loop feedback system for real-time mechanical targeting in dynamic nanofiber scaffolds
ideas.description: This redesigned approach creates an integrated platform where the evolving viscoelastic properties of a dynamically cross-linked nanofiber scaffold are measured in real-time using non-destructive, embedded sensing techniques. A computational control algorithm processes this live mechanical data and automatically adjusts the application of cross-linking triggers (such as light intensity or chemical influx) to steer the network towards a user-defined mechanical target, such as a specific storage modulus or stress relaxation profile. This closed-loop system moves beyond static optimization, enabling adaptive, on-demand mechanical control for applications like simulating dynamic tissue environments or guiding stem cell differentiation through precisely timed mechanical cues.

ideas.title: Bioresponsive, Self-Paced Degradation via Closed-Loop Biochemical Feedback
ideas.description: This approach engineers an implant material whose disassembly is not governed by a predetermined timeline, but is directly triggered and modulated by the local biochemical environment produced during active bone formation. The material incorporates labile chemical bonds or encapsulants that are specifically cleaved by enzymes (e.g., matrix metalloproteinases, alkaline phosphatase) or pH changes characteristic of the bone remodeling front. As new bone tissue forms and releases these signals, the adjacent implant material degrades in a spatially and temporally coupled manner. This ensures the structural support recedes only as fast as the native tissue replaces it, inherently matching the patient's unique healing trajectory and eliminating the risk of rate mismatch.

ideas.description: This system creates a living, adaptive microenvironment by combining initial photolithographic patterning of IKVAV epitopes with a capacity for subsequent, guided pattern erosion. A secondary, cell-degradable matrix contains a photocaged molecule that competitively disrupts IKVAV-integrin interactions. Using real-time microscopy feedback on cell behavior, targeted UV light can be applied to uncage this inhibitor in specific regions, dynamically reducing effective ligand density. This allows the pattern of epitope presentation to evolve in direct response to cellular activity, more accurately mimicking developmental and regenerative processes.

ideas.title: Programmed Material Phase-Out via Environmental Feedback
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from ecological succession in soil restoration, where pioneer plant species stabilize an environment and then senesce as the conditions become suitable for target species, this idea adapts the concept of phased, environmentally-triggered removal to biomaterials. The analogous solution uses species with lifecycles timed to environmental feedback. Adapted to bone regeneration, the research idea is to engineer a multi-component scaffold where controlled disassembly is not governed by a single timeline, but by sequential degradation of different material phases in direct response to specific biochemical markers of the healing process (e.g., inflammatory cytokines, then osteogenic enzymes). This matches the scaffold's resorption profile not just to the rate, but to the specific stages of new bone formation, ensuring mechanical support is withdrawn only as the native tissue can assume the load.

ideas.description: This redesigned system uses a liquid composite precursor formulated to undergo ultra-fast, initial physical gelation upon contact with blood and physiological ions, preventing washout. This first gel network is inherently hemostatic and provides immediate structural containment within the irregular defect. Subsequently, a second, independent chemical reaction within the stabilized gel triggers controlled mineralization, mimicking the natural process of bone formation. This sequential approach ensures complete defect filling and robust integration, even in challenging clinical environments with active bleeding.

ideas.title: Sparse, Patterned Epitope Presentation Inspired by Visual Acuity Testing
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that high, uniform epitope density is necessary for selective cellular differentiation by drawing an analogy from visual acuity testing in ophthalmology. In that field, the discriminatory power of the visual system is tested using sparse, patterned cues (like the gaps in Landolt C rings) rather than solid blocks of stimulus. Adapting this solution, the research would test if immune cell selectivity can be achieved by presenting target epitopes in specific, sub-maximal geometric patterns (e.g., arrays of dots or lines) on a substrate. The core hypothesis is that cells integrate signals from these discrete, strategically placed points, potentially reaching the threshold for a specific differentiation pathway while using significantly less ligand material than a high-density coating, thereby testing the necessity of high epitope density.

ideas.title: Quantify signaling threshold for selective T cell activation on sub-maximal epitope patterns
ideas.description: This research integrates engineered antigen-presenting surfaces with controlled, patterned epitope densities and live-cell imaging of downstream T cell signaling reporters. The goal is to establish a functional map linking specific spatial and density parameters of epitope presentation to quantifiable signaling outputs, such as NFAT translocation or sustained calcium oscillations. This approach will define the minimum signaling 'dose' required for productive differentiation (e.g., to effector function) versus the sub-threshold signaling that leads to anergy or altered fate. The system thus tests the necessity of high density by directly probing the signaling sufficiency of patterned, sub-maximal presentations.
